夜雨秋灯录 - Records of Autumn Lamps in Night Rain

    Home
    1

    夜雨秋灯录
    　　
    　　清　宣鼎　著
    　　
    　　
    　　先正有言曰：读书忌老，著书忌早。鼎既服膺而敢违之乎！缘鼎少膺孱弱，壮值乱离，濒于死者再，而皆得不死，故有是录耳。先慈诞鼎之前夕．梦一道者来叩首滕下，已而生．鼎口不茹荤者十九年．性好佛老，闻人有谈玄者，听之忘倦，而尤爱仆媪说果报鬼怪逸事。年十一习楷．书扁额、屏幛．居然挥洒。十五解为文，十九忽膺咯血疾．惫矣．旋得《感应编图说》读之，获瘳。二十外先慈见背，先嗣父广文公又见背。

    Explanation

    1

    The Night Rain and Autumn Lamp Records
    　　
    　　By Xuan Ding of the Qing Dynasty
    　　
    　　
    　　The ancients once said: "Reading should be avoided in old age, and writing should be avoided in youth." Having accepted this advice, how dare I go against it! Because I was weak in my youth and faced turmoil in my prime, I nearly died twice, but survived both times, hence this record. The night before my mother gave birth to me, she dreamed of a Taoist coming to bow at the door, and then I was born. I abstained from meat for nineteen years, had a fondness for Buddhism and Taoism, and when I hear people talking about the profound, I listen without tiring, especially loving stories told by servants and maids about karma, ghosts, and strange events. At eleven, I practiced calligraphy on plaques, screens, and banners, and could write freely. At fifteen, I began to write essays. At nineteen, I suddenly suffered from hemoptysis, becoming very weak. Soon after, I read Induction Compilation with Illustrations and recovered. After twenty, my mother passed away, and my stepfather, the scholar, also passed away.

    2

    家难既起，外侮乘之，袅獍成群，争噬吾肉，家道遂中落．年廿四遇歉岁，独卧枯寺，中饿几毙，旋得金经《莲花经》讽之．机始转。廿六奉生父命赘入外家，方得延残喘。明年突红巾至，携家窜东海，慷慨从军，几死锋镝．旋又回海上卖画供饘粥。年三十一始入当道幕司笔札，三十五馆淮海，三十九游山左，奔波寒涩．近于托钵矣。明年入兖郡滋阳署，郡有少陵台，峨峨百尺，古墟也。时九月将近，友人约登台望曲阜，西风逼人，黄花满地，鼎偶问友曰：“今日何日？”曰：“廿八。

    Explanation

    2

    After family troubles arose, external attacks took advantage of the situation. Evil people gathered in groups, fiercely attacking my resources, and my family's fortunes declined to a low point. At the age of twenty-four, I encountered a year of famine, and I lay alone in a desolate temple, nearly dying from hunger. I soon obtained the precious Lotus Sutra and recited it, and my fate began to change. At the age of twenty-six, I married into my mother's family at the command of my living father, and I was able to prolong my life. The following year, the Red Turban rebels arrived, and I fled to the East Sea with my family. I bravely joined the army and nearly died in battle. I soon returned to the sea and sold paintings to provide for my daily needs. At the age of thirty-one, I began to serve in an official capacity, drafting documents for the ruling party. At the age of thirty-five, I resided in Huaihai, and at the age of thirty-nine, I traveled in Shandong. I traveled frequently and lived in poverty, and I have nearly become like a monk begging for alms. The following year, I entered the office in Ziyang County, Yan Prefecture. The prefecture has the Shao Ling Terrace, a tall, ancient ruin, a hundred feet high. At the time, the ninth month was approaching, and a friend invited me to climb the terrace and look toward Qufu. The west wind was chilling, and the ground was covered with chrysanthemums. "What day is today?" asked "Dingou." "The twenty-eighth."

    3

    ”鼎不禁大惊，继而大恸，盖此日即贱辰，忽忽焉行年已四十矣，而沦落犹是乎！归则僵卧不语，亦不哭，明日遂病且殆，精神倘侊，不知所云。病十五日，忽蹶然起，裁笺为阄，取生平目所见，耳所闻，心所记忆且深信者，仿稗官例．先书一百余目，每夕作文一篇，或两篇，不数日而患遂霍然。明年解馆．赁宅任城，售书卖画，甫稍有余。居两年，回秦邮游虎阜，计得文一百一十五篇，皆初稿而未及修饰者。客有索观既竟，莫不啧啧曰：“太早，太早。”鼎曰：”余深凛忌早之诫，然余之文．刀圭也．未敢以著述论。

    Explanation

    3

    鼎不禁大惊，继而大恸，盖此日即贱辰，忽忽焉行年已四十矣，而沦落犹是乎！归则僵卧不语，亦不哭，明日遂病且殆，精神倘侊，不知所云。病十五日，忽蹶然起，裁笺为阄，取生平目所见，耳所闻，心所记忆且深信者，仿稗官例．先书一百余目，每夕作文一篇，或两篇，不数日而患遂霍然。明年解馆．赁宅任城，售书卖画，甫稍有余。居两年，回秦邮游虎阜，计得文一百一十五篇，皆初稿而未及修饰者。客有索观既竟，莫不啧啧曰：“太早，太早。”鼎曰：”余深凛忌早之诫，然余之文．刀圭也．未敢以著述论。

    4

    ”客问：“夜雨秋灯四字作何解？”曰：“当其病滋阳署时．愁霖滴沥，冷焰动摇，千里家山，时入梦寐，秋魂欲语，病魔乍来，此无可奈何之境也。以无可奈何之身．当无可奈何之境，未能已已，奋笔直书耳！”甲戌之冬，有仙蝶过访，是夕设乩于室，以文呈．须臾，仙至，书曰“子来前！子前身为罗浮冲虚观道士．以弄笔头获过，今又弄笔耶！幸语类荒唐，实严瘅赏，且无淫词，是尚可恕。再四十三年，当于忍辱班中迟子矣！”鼎稽首谢，求仙人序．不可，遂自述颠末如此。噫，樵歌牧唱有时上献刍荛，鬼董孤谐无语不关讽劝。尘之封，蠹之蚀，举而抛同落叶，忍乎哉！ 时光绪三年春二月花朝日，天长宣鼎瘦梅自序于仙蝶来馆。

    Explanation

    4

    The guest asked, "What does the phrase 'night rain and autumn lamp' mean?" The response was, "When I was sick in the hot summer, the sorrowful raindrops fell, the cold flame flickered, the distant mountains and rivers of my homeland often appeared in my dreams, the autumn spirit wished to speak, but the disease demon suddenly came, this was a state of helplessness. With an helpless body, facing an helpless situation, unable to stop, I wrote with great effort!" In the winter of the year Jia Xu, a fairy butterfly visited, and that night, a divination board was set up in the room, and a text was presented. Soon, the fairy arrived and wrote, "Come here! Your previous life was a Taoist priest at the Luofu Chongxu Temple. You gained recognition through your writing, and now you are writing again! Fortunately, your words are somewhat absurd, truly deserving of severe praise, and there are no obscene words, so you may be forgiven. In forty-three years, you will be delayed in the class of those who endure humiliation!" The author bowed and thanked, requesting a preface from the fairy. The fairy refused, so the author recounted the events in this manner. Alas, the songs of woodcutters and shepherds sometimes offer humble advice, and the harmonious ghosts and spirits speak without fail in admonition. Dust covers and worms eat, and all is thrown away like fallen leaves, how can one endure this! On the day of the flower festival in the second month of spring in the third year of the Guangxu era, Tianchang Xuanding Shoumei self-introduced at the Fairy Butterfly Pavilion.

    5

    卷一
    　　
    　　青天白日
    　　
    　　浙人南宫认庵，以字行，幼随父琥宦于粤，清廉，穷其橐。母先逝，父继卒。亏库款，将系狱。素审叔璧幕于苏，欲往依之。潜焚两亲骸，以竹笼负之，徒步逸。餐风咽露，跋涉奔波，一载始抵。寻叔无耗。时苏正岁歉，益困急。倾守囊钱，购半亩地，瘗之，志以碑，结茅若团瓢，守其侧。蓬头垢面，沦入乞儿，剩饭残羹，尚知荐祭。时年仅三五，性孝且慧，貌癯不枯。闻吴儿山歌，学之，即当伍氏箫，始免馁。朝村暮郭，倏忽三年。偶倚古寺门，向阳扪虱。

    Explanation

    5

    Volume One

    Blue Sky and Bright Sun

    A man from Zhejiang, Nan Gong Ren'an, was known by his courtesy name. As a young boy, he followed his father, who was an official in Guangdong. His father was clean and honest, but his purse was empty. His mother died first, followed by his father. He incurred a debt to the treasury and was about to be imprisoned. He had always known that his uncle served in a government office in Suzhou and wanted to go and rely on him. He secretly burned the remains of his parents, placed them in a bamboo cage, and carried them away on foot. He ate wind and drank dew, traveling arduously. After a year, he finally arrived. He searched for his uncle but found no trace of him. At that time, Suzhou was suffering from a poor harvest, and he became even more desperate. He spent the last of his money to buy half an acre of land, buried the remains, marked the site with a stone tablet, and built a thatched hut nearby to guard it. He became a beggar, unkempt and dirty, but still knew to offer leftover food and soup as sacrifices. He was only three or five years old, yet he was filial and intelligent, with a thin but not emaciated appearance. He heard the mountain songs of the Wu people, learned them, and played the flute of the Wu clan, thus avoiding starvation. He lived in the village in the morning and the town in the evening, and so it was three years. One day, he leaned against the door of an ancient temple, facing the sun, and picked lice from his clothes.

    6

    面即贵家园，时见美人楼上眺。顷一小环，艳年二八，出而反掩其门，行向西；忽伏草际，少时，整衣去。知为小遗。甫数步，即若有物坠地，软无声；呼告之，婢不觉。遂掩襟往观，锦袱也。中裹金玉钗钏，珠宝零星，媵以鱼函，折作方胜。书云：“十郎哥哥足下：妹谬以陋质，获配清芬；亲上做亲，幸中之幸。牵牛西北，一水盈盈；孔雀东南，双声隐隐。盟深金石，妹喜嫁得梁鸿；劫转沧桑，郎忽贫如司马。然而鲍宣对挽鹿车，阮氏何嫌犊鼻。幸鸡窗攻苦，卜鸿路飞腾。

    Explanation

    6

    Facing the noble garden, I often see a beautiful woman gazing from the upper floor. A moment later, a young woman of sixteen or seventeen came out, closed the door behind her, and walked westward; suddenly she crouched in the grass. After a while, she combed her clothes and left. It was known to be a small gift. Just a few steps away, something seemed to fall to the ground, soft and without sound; I called out to her, but the maid did not notice. Then I covered my chest and went to look. It was a brocade cloth. Inside, it was wrapped with gold and jade hairpins and bracelets, scattered with jewels, accompanied by a fish-shaped box, folded into a square pattern. The letter said:

    "Dear Brother Shilang: Sister, with my humble qualities, I was fortunate to be matched with your noble fragrance. A match made by parents, a happiness within happiness. The cowherd in the northwest, a clear stream flows; peacocks in the southeast, their sounds faintly heard. Our vows are as firm as stone, sister, I am delighted to have married a man like Liang Hong. The world has changed, and suddenly you are poor like Sima. However, Bo Xuan pulled the ox cart, and the Ruan family had no objections to the ox cart. May you study hard at the chicken window, and predict a soaring career."

    7

    敢怨标梅，抚青春而未艾；唯祈折桂，脱白袷以来迎。逆知青眼频更，红丝欲断。每听狐语，似怨前度之冰；欲倩蜂媒，再接他家之酒。心石坚而不转，辞簧鼓而须防。若真挟瑟改弦，定弹黄鹄；时拟传笺布意，恨少青鸾。小婢娟奴，虽曰主仆，实同腹心。事已迫于燃眉，情实殷于刺目。遣寻一鹗，面展双鱼。奉上缄泪之句，断肠之辞，婉转十三行，预仿苏娘织锦；附以缠臂之金，搔头之玉，珍珠一百颗，聊为匡壁添光。不尽缠绵，泥中人再为哓舌；未敢陨谢，爨下材急不择音。

    Explanation

    7

    Daring to blame the plum blossom, cherishing the prime of youth while it is still unexhausted; only praying to pluck the osmanthus, shedding the white robe and coming to welcome. Anticipating the frequent change of favor, the red thread is about to be severed. Often hearing the fox's words, seeming to complain about the previous ice; wishing to ask the bee as a matchmaker, to reconnect with another family's wine. The heart is as hard as stone and does not turn, avoiding the flattery of the reed pipe. If truly to take up the zither and change the strings, to surely play the song of the yellow crane; sometimes planning to convey one's intentions through letters, regretting the lack of the green phoenix. The young maid Jua, though called master and servant, is truly like a close confidant. The matter is pressing like a fire under the eyebrows, and the feelings are intense like a thorn in the eye. Sending to seek a hawk, to present two fish in person. Presenting the sealed, tearful lines, the heart-wrenching words, the graceful thirteen lines, anticipating to imitate Su Niang's brocade; attaching the arm-wrapping gold, the head-scratching jade, one hundred pearls, to slightly add luster to the wall. The endless entanglement, the person in the mud speaks again; not daring to decline, the material under the stove is urgent and does not choose the sound.

    8

    敢布愚忱，伏惟怜鉴。某年月日。秦氏小妹贞璞。裣衽手肃。”南惊读一过，诧曰：“险哉！东床落魄，富儿赖婚，钟情者越礼冒嫌，进疗贫方。若为他人捡得，则婢固命尽，即迢迢牛女，亦复睽违，鹊桥无日矣！曷坐以觇之。”少顷，婢返，面色灰死，仓皇觅榛莽间，不得，仰天叹曰：“奴死不足惜，负主人托，奈何？”南笑曰：“娘行失何物，值何言死？”婢闻其言有因，哀祈曰：“好男子，曾寓目否？”曰：“卿能明告我，或者璧能返也。”曰：“我秦氏婢娟奴，日伴女公子。

    Explanation

    8

    Daring to express my humble sincerity, I humbly hope for your compassion and understanding. On the day of the month. Qin's younger sister Zhenpu. With folded hands, she respectfully presents herself. South read it once and was startled, saying, "How dangerous! The man on the east bed is penniless, the wealthy rely on marriage, those deeply in love violate propriety and risk disgrace, and offer a remedy for poverty. If someone else picks it up, then the maid's fate is sealed, even the distant Niulang and Zhinv will be separated, and the magpie bridge will never come again! Why not sit and watch to see what happens?" A short while later, the maid returned, her face ashen and dead, frantically searching the thicket, but could not find it. She looked up to heaven and sighed, "My death is not worth pitying, but I have betrayed my master's trust, what shall I do?" South laughed and said, "What did you lose, and why speak of death?" The maid heard that he had a reason, and begged sorrowfully, "A good man, did you see him?" He said, "If you can clearly tell me, perhaps the jade can be returned." She said, "I am the maid Juannü of the Qin family, who accompanies the young lady every day."

    9

    主见婿家贫，欲改适，女公子日夜哭。妾怜之，请以妆台中旧蓄者约五百金，裹以绞绡，附以雁字，亲去作寄书邮，付于小檀郎。嘱入都，谋战捷，好亲迎。堕其物，必泄其谋，乌能不悲！”言已大恸。曰：“然则将若何？”曰：“死耳！”南探怀与之，曰：“是耶？非耶？”婢见即伏地叩，南挽之，示殷勤。婢曰：“子乞人，得暴富，弃之甘乎？妾将何以报？”曰：“报我不难，恐我之所易，子之所难也；我之所甘，子之所苦也。”曰：“试言之。

    Explanation

    9

    The lady saw that the groom's family was poor and wanted to change the marriage plans. The noblewoman cried day and night. The maid pitied her and asked to take out about five hundred gold pieces from the old savings in the dressing table, wrap them in fine silk, and accompany them with a letter in the form of a crane's call. She would go personally to send the letter and hand it over to the young master. She instructed him to go to the capital, seek victory in battle, and then come to fetch her in person. If the package falls, the plan will be exposed, how can one not be sad? She said this and wept bitterly. The lady asked, "Then what should we do?" The maid replied, "There is no other way but to die!" The lady took something out of her bosom and gave it to her, saying, "Is it this? or not?" The maid immediately fell to the ground and bowed. The lady pulled her up, showing her kindness. The maid said, "You, a beggar who became suddenly rich, would you be willing to abandon her? How will I repay you?" The lady replied, "Reporting back to me is not difficult, but what is easy for me might be difficult for you; what I find sweet, you might find bitter." The maid said, "Try to explain."

    10

    ”曰：“仆虽冠，犹童子身；子貌美极矣，未知能令我真个销魂否？”婢赧然，徐报曰：“君姑待我，行行即来。”婢赍金玉去，南亦出东郭。三日后，偶经园外，远闻莺声，唤曰：“来乎！”仰视之，婢也。笑而招以手。门砉然开，南遽掩入。湖山石畔，芳草如茵。婢曰：“以此聊报大德，可一不可再。”南曰：“诺。”方将偎抱，见婢以红罗盖粉面。南笑曰：“子秀色可餐，方欣饱看，博须臾欢，何吝而抑藏乎？亦娇羞故态乎？”婢以纤指向上曰：“青天白日，不怕神明耶？”南蓦如棒喝，情魔潜逋。

    Explanation

    10

    He said, "Although I have come of age, I am still like a child; your appearance is extremely beautiful, but I wonder if you can truly make me lose my heart." The servant blushed and slowly replied, "Please wait for me, I will come soon." The servant carried away with gold and jade, and Nan also went out of the eastern suburb. Three days later, he happened to pass by outside the garden and heard the sound of orioles from afar. He called out, "Are you coming?" Looking up, he saw the servant. She smiled and beckoned with her hand. The door opened with a loud noise, and Nan quickly entered. By the lake, by the stones, and with lush grass like a carpet, the servant said, "To slightly repay your great kindness, once is enough, not twice." Nan said, "Yes." Just about to embrace, he saw the servant covering her face with a red silk veil. Nan laughed and said, "Your beauty is so exquisite that it can be eaten; I was just about to enjoy looking at you fully, to gain a brief moment of joy, why be stingy and hide it? Is this just your usual shy and modest behavior?" The servant pointed upward with her slender fingers and said, "Under the clear blue sky and bright sun, are you not afraid of the gods?" Nan suddenly felt as if struck by a stick, and the demon of passion fled secretly.

    11

    遽起，曰：“子畏神明，仆宁不畏乎？”持竿携篮，喃喃诵“青天白日”四字，出门去。婢遥遥致声：“君每日日中来，当分己食果君腹。”南瞑目狂奔，亦不计园门闭否。明日市上行，有一相者，呼曰：“丐者来！子卧蚕下，何来此阴纹？应在三十六日，必有非常遇。”曰：“吾日唱莲花求生活，免沟壑足矣，尚欲追踪荥阳公子耶？”曰：“不然，验当酬若干？”曰：“十千。诬则若何？”曰：“抉我双眸子。”曰：“先生眸子危矣。”至三十五日，丐如故，走告相者曰：“眸子权寄尊庞一宵稳。

    Explanation

    11

    The beggar suddenly stood up and said, "If you fear the divine, how can I not fear it too?" He carried his fishing pole and basket, muttering the phrase "clear sky and bright sun" four times, and then left. From a distance, the servant called out, "If you come every day at noon, I will share my food with you." The beggar ran blindly and frantically, not caring whether the garden gate was open or closed. The next day, as he was going to the market, he met a fortune-teller who shouted, "The beggar is here! Your eyes below the cheeks, why do you have this unusual mark? On the thirty-sixth day, you will surely have an extraordinary encounter." The beggar replied, "I sing the lotus every day to make a living, just to avoid poverty. Do I still want to follow the noble deeds of the Prince of Yingyang?" The fortune-teller asked, "If the prediction is wrong, what will you pay?" The beggar said, "Ten thousand coins. What if it's false?" The fortune-teller replied, "I will gouge out my eyes." The beggar said, "Your eyes are in danger, sir." By the thirty-fifth day, the beggar remained unchanged. He went to inform the fortune-teller, "I will keep your eyes safe for one night, sir."

    12

    ”相者又视之，拍掌曰：“得矣！边城紫气透，当先得财。”南曰：“诺。”明午步长街，忽有牵其裾者，呼曰：“月儿耶？”南颈有月牙痕，故小字月。视其人，鲜衣怒马，貌若贵官，姑应曰：“然。”其人惨涕，曰：“儿何至此？”南蓦忆曰：“璧叔耶？”曰：“然。儿盍随之归寓？”庐中起居华灿，叩阿婶，亦甚慰。因缕述颠末，哭失声。叔曰：“久审尔父母殁，特函询，无回字，乃在此也。吾年老殷富，无子息，今得犹子，不愁继续矣！”立命婢媪，为更褴褛，且濯香泉，依旧翩翩，颜色焕发。

    Explanation

    12

    The diviner observed again, clapping his hands and exclaiming, "Found it! The purple aura from the border city is clear, indicating that one will first acquire wealth." Nan replied, "Yes." The next day at noon, he was walking along the main street when suddenly someone grabbed his hem and called out, "Moon, is that you?" Nan had a crescent-shaped mark on his neck, which was why he was nicknamed "Moon." He looked at the person, who was dressed in bright, new clothes and riding a spirited horse, appearing like a high-ranking official. He tentatively responded, "Yes." The person wept bitterly and said, "How did you end up here?" Nan suddenly remembered and asked, "Uncle Bi?" "Yes," the person replied. "Why don't you follow me home?" In the luxurious and splendid house, Nan asked his aunt, who was also very relieved. He then recounted the entire story in detail, losing his voice in tears. His uncle said, "I had long ago learned that your parents had passed away and specially wrote to inquire, but received no reply. That's why you're here. I am old, wealthy, and childless. Now that I have an adopted son, I no longer have to worry about continuing the family line!" He immediately commanded the maids and servants to change Nan into ragged clothes and bathe him in fragrant spring water. Afterward, Nan remained graceful and radiant in appearance.

    13

    婉述相士神，叔命以十千酬之，欲招致为叔鉴慈颜，不许。旬余，忽以千金付之，曰：“儿既废读，当学贾。”以齿稚辞。曰：“试为之，无不利。”南束装买棹，过江运白粲，大获。明年今日归省叔，至则门户犹存，第主非是。询居停，曰：“子去后，尔叔亦他徙，且不知徙何处。”南茫茫无适从，因思江北新置宅，曷归而侦访，扫墓瞻仰。榜人频催，腰缠馀五百金，即全购柏油压船。渡后，北风怒号，江冻十日，不能解。柏油值顿昂，获利可十倍。

    Explanation

    13

    Wan Shu, a renowned diviner, was invited by his uncle with an offer of ten thousand coins. The uncle wished to bring him in to gain his approval and kindness, but Wan Shu refused. After a few days, the uncle suddenly gave him a thousand gold pieces and said, "Since you have abandoned your studies, you should learn to trade." Wan Shu declined, citing his young age. The uncle encouraged him, saying, "Try it; there will be no harm." Wan Shu then packed his belongings and hired a boat, crossing the river to transport a type of grain, and made a significant profit. On this day the following year, he returned home to visit his uncle. Upon arrival, he found the door and gate still standing, but the master of the house was no longer the same. He asked the host, who informed him, "After you left, your uncle also moved elsewhere, and I don't know where he went." Wan Shu was lost and unsure of what to do, so he thought about his newly purchased house north of the river. Why not return there to investigate and pay respects? The boatman frequently urged him to hurry. With 500 gold pieces remaining in his belt, he immediately bought all the pitch needed to caulk the boat. After crossing, the north wind howled fiercely, and the river froze for ten days, unable to thaw. The price of pitch suddenly increased, and the profit could be ten times greater.

    14

    更于宅前设缎铺，以三千金为母，招老成者四五辈，司出纳。又一年，心不忘叔，一人渡江，搭航船，至中流，风大作，浪如山立，霹雳大震。同侪者十数人，咸见云中现极大四金字，曰：“青天白日，”笔画分明。众诵佛禳之，而字仍现。雷复鸣，船几裂。众急曰：“上天已明示恶人，事各自省，无累他人！”南挺身告众曰：“此仆隐事，诚不可以告人，敢相累乎！”急向怒流，踊身投。昏懵中，抱得枯槎，听其掀簸。耳听雷雨骤下，火光窜金蛇。须臾霁，视此身如一点萍，乘万里浪也。

    Explanation

    14

    The protagonist changed and set up a silk shop in front of his house, taking three thousand gold coins as the capital. He hired several experienced elders to manage the accounting. After a year, he could not forget his uncle. Alone, he crossed the river, boarded a ferry, and when he reached midstream, the wind suddenly rose, the waves as high as mountains, and thunder roared. Several dozen fellow travelers all saw four large golden characters appear in the clouds, saying "Blue Sky and White Sun." The strokes were clearly defined. The crowd chanted Buddhist prayers to ward it off, but the characters still appeared. The thunder roared again, and the boat nearly split. The crowd hastily said, "Heaven has clearly shown the evil person, let each reflect on their own deeds, and do not burden others!" Nan stood up and said to the crowd, "This is my hidden matter, truly not something to tell others, dare I burden you?" He quickly turned toward the raging current and leaped in. In a dazed state, he grabbed a piece of driftwood and let it toss him about. He heard the thunder and rain suddenly pour down, and the lightning flashed like golden snakes. After a short while, the storm cleared, and he saw himself like a single leaf of duckweed, riding the waves for miles.

    15

    忽一官舫鸣锣挂帆来，呼曰：“速救活抱槎人！赏十贯！”旋来红船，挈而登，官舫人非他，叔也。互惊。问：“叔何处来？”曰：“吾移居通州，偶游紫琅山耳。尔父母墓无恙，尔之心曲，吾已悉。娟奴已随秦家女适婿家。婿果贵，即迎娶。尔事缘未至，毋躁也！”同之通寓，见婶安好，奴婢更多于苏寓。不敢问。居二日，袖出小折呈叔，曰：“此年来子金总目也。”曰：“烦甚，曷携去。”翌晨辞别，叔又赠以数百金。抵苏，访娟，果如叔言。

    Explanation

    15

    Suddenly, an official boat sounded gongs and raised its sails, arriving with a call: "Quickly rescue the living person clinging to the log! Reward ten strings of cash!" Soon, a red boat arrived, and the person was lifted onto it. The people on the official boat were not others but the narrator's uncle. Both were startled. The narrator asked, "Uncle, where have you come from?" The uncle replied, "I have moved to Tongzhou and happened to visit Zilang Mountain. Your parents' tomb is safe, and I understand your inner thoughts. Juan has followed the Qin family's daughter to her husband's home. If the son-in-law becomes wealthy, she will be formally married. Your time has not yet come, do not be impatient!" The narrator stayed with the uncle, who was safe, and the household had more servants than at the previous residence. The narrator dared not ask. After two days, the narrator discreetly presented a small folded document to the uncle, saying, "This is a summary of the gold and silver I have earned this year." The uncle said, "It is very troublesome, why don't you take it with you." The next morning, they bid farewell, and the uncle gave several hundred strings of cash as a gift. Upon arriving in Suzhou, the narrator visited Juan, and it was as the uncle said.

    16

    忽遇弄船人，惊曰：“君尚生耶？若辈皆震死，船覆，吾抱缆姑免。顷泊此修治耳。”南出资赠助之。即假馆舟子家。偶倚白板闲眺，见一美人，乘香舆，随仆媪；复又一婢，乘下泽车，貌酷似娟。尾行三四里，至一尼庵，美人登殿拜如来，仆媪憩游廊，庵主献香茗。婢闲步，偶见南，目荧荧低唤曰：“青天白日。”南失声曰：“噫！娟娘耶？”婢问：“何遽华洁？”告以故。曰：“钟情者，尚恋旧耶？”曰：“中怀无一刻忘。”曰：“彼此有情，尚不审姓氏，令人齿冷。”又详告之。旋闻内呼娟，美人偕去。

    Explanation

    16

    Suddenly, the narrator met a boatman, who exclaimed in surprise: "Are you still alive? All of you were killed by the earthquake, the boat capsized, and I held onto the cable and barely escaped. I just stopped here to make repairs." The narrator contributed money to help the boatman. He immediately rented a room in the boatman's house. Occasionally leaning against a white board to gaze idly, the narrator saw a beautiful woman riding in a fragrant sedan chair, followed by servants. Another maid, riding in a lower-class carriage, had an appearance very similar to Juan. She followed for three or four miles, arriving at a nunnery. The beautiful woman climbed the steps to bow before the Buddha, while the servants rested in the corridor. The abbess offered fragrant tea. The maid walked idly and happened to see the narrator. Her eyes flickered, and she whispered softly, "Blue sky and bright day." The narrator exclaimed, "Ah! Is it you, Juan?" The maid asked, "Why are you so refined and clean?" The narrator told the reason. She said, "Those who are in love still miss the old days?" The narrator replied, "Not a moment has passed without thinking of you." She said, "If we both have feelings for each other, but don't even know each other's names, it makes me shiver." The narrator explained in detail. Soon after, she heard someone calling Juan, and the beautiful woman left together.

    17

    南惆怅无聊赖，踟蹰丛葬处，见一大冢，碑云：“东浙寓公南宫讳璧玉人先生之墓”。志文详叙夫妻同卒于苏，死近五载，旅葬于此，以待犹子南宫认庵他日寻觅。撰书者，里诸生郁访。南读毕大惊。回忆叔婶面目，不知此墓中人，何既同名氏，且犹子亦同名，必无是理，而竟有是事。归访文举士，知郁访第，具冠投刺入拜。憬然曰：“君颈有月牙痕乎？”曰：“然。”曰：“令尊叔甫生时，与先大夫莫逆交。

    17

    Nan, filled with melancholy and aimlessness, hesitated at the burial ground, where he saw a large tomb. The stele read, “The tomb of Mr. Nan Gong, courtesy name Bi Yu Ren, a guest from the eastern region of Zhejiang.” The inscription detailed that the husband and wife had both died in Suzhou, nearly five years ago, and were buried here as temporary guests, awaiting their nephew, Nan Gong Ren An, to come and search for them in the future. The inscription was written by a local scholar named Yu Fan. After reading it, Nan was greatly surprised. Recalling the appearance of his uncle and aunt, he wondered how it could be that the person in the tomb had the same name and surname as his uncle and aunt, and even had a nephew with the same name. It must not be true, yet there was indeed such a thing. He returned to visit a scholar named Wenju, learned the residence of Yu Fan, and, wearing a hat and presenting a card, entered to pay respects. Suddenly enlightened, he asked, “Do you have a crescent-shaped mark on your neck?” The other person replied, “Yes.” Nan then said, “Your father, Shu Fu, was born at the same time as my late father, and they were close friends.”

    18

    先大夫逝，叔甫老伉俪亦相继逝。易篑前数日，坚嘱鄙人预后事，谋吉壤，作墓志；并嘱君如回苏，即为继续。君从何知有仆，而惠然肯来耶？”南以见墓志对。曰：“幸不负先人遗命也。”南蹙额以两次遇叔告，求解惑。曰：“令叔甫生习吐纳，殁能解脱。如子言，其仙去乎？”南遣人如通访叔，杳矣。遂移两骨函，依叔墓，重葬。双冢峨峨，加以封植，手自撰文，勒石纪事。访视之，喜曰：“子尚能习举子业，何自弃？”盖访已前科北闱中经魁，因款留，教之读。

    Explanation

    18

    The late father passed away, and Uncle Fu's elderly wife also passed away in succession. A few days before his passing, he firmly instructed me to prepare for the after-death arrangements, to find a good burial site, and to write a tombstone inscription. He also instructed that if you return to Suzhou, you should continue. How did you know about me, and why are you so kind as to come? I replied about the tombstone inscription. "I am fortunate to not have failed the late father's last wishes." I frowned and told him about the two encounters with Uncle, seeking to resolve my confusion. "He practiced breathing exercises in life and could release himself upon death. As you say, did he ascend to immortality?" I sent someone to Suzhou to look for Uncle, but he was nowhere to be found. So I moved the two coffins to Uncle's tomb and reburied them. The two tombs stood tall, and I added a fence and planted trees, personally writing an inscription and carving it on stone to record the events. Upon visiting them, I was pleased to say, "You can still study the Confucian classics, why have you given up?" It turned out that he had already passed the imperial examination in the northern region and was the top scholar, so I invited him to stay and teach me to read.

    19

    是秋回浙中副车，访开筵为贺，鼓吹大作，泥金署墙，把酒向南曰：“君贵矣，芳年二十有一，尚欲歌朝飞雉耶？”曰：“弟有旧盟，痴心拱候耳。”曰：“愚兄代觅佳人，为君权署县君，何如？”言未已，即有婢媪扶一美人，出与交拜，南不知所措，挽之拜，两行画烛送入洞房，亲为反扃双闼，临去顾内曰：“今宵好报恩，毋再怨我夫妇累汝。”漏静，揭巾微睨，则红粉露垂，香肩玉削，颇似娟，亦低唤曰：“青天白日。”新人微粲曰：“闷葫芦打破矣！”南闻之大喜，始知尼寺美人即妻，当日寄书人也。夫贵，娶已两载，极敦爱。

    Explanation

    19

    In that autumn, returning to Zhejiang as the deputy vehicle, he visited and opened a banquet in celebration. The music and drums were played loudly, and he wrote with gold ink on the wall. Holding wine and facing south, he said, "You are now honored, at the age of twenty-one, still wishing to sing about the morning flying pheasant?" The subject replied, "I have an old alliance, and I am waiting with a foolish heart." "My elder brother will find a good woman for you, and temporarily appoint her as a county lady, how about that?" Before he could finish speaking, a maid and an old servant brought out a beautiful woman, and they bowed to each other. Nan was at a loss, and led her to bow. Two rows of painted candles escorted them into the bridal chamber, and he personally locked the double doors. As he was leaving, he looked inside and said, "Tonight is a good time to repay the debt, do not blame my wife and me for burdening you again." As the night grew quiet, he lifted the veil and glanced. Her red makeup drooped, her fragrant shoulders were slender, and she looked somewhat like Juan, and she also called out softly, "Blue sky and bright sun." The new bride smiled faintly and said, "The puzzle has been solved!" Nan heard this and was greatly delighted, realizing that the beautiful woman from the temple was his wife, the one who had sent him a letter that day. When he became honored, he had been married for two years, and they were deeply affectionate.

    20

    娟重见南，始为贞述南来访始末，贞为访述。副车之中，赖访力夤缘关节，始获隽。其所以不即以娟与之者，恐误读耳。少顷，入帏绸缪，犹处子。明日谢，亦谢。至是始道破。曰：“君遇遗金不拾，仆亦留全璧以待也。”从此秦越一家，亲爱如手足。南旋即援例谒选，官扬州司马，携娟娘去。颇不自讳，每谓僚佐曰：“不图黄榜中，亦有卑田院中乞儿也！”懊侬氏曰：昔披裘人云：“仆岂拾人遗金者哉？”然高人遇之易，乞人遇之不易。至美色当前，悬崖勒马，此等功夫，尤不易。伟哉南宫！宜乎苍苍者，报以厚德也。吾愿普天下男儿，无论富贵贫贱，当人人书“青天白日”四字于座右。

    Explanation

    20

    Juan saw South again, and began to recount to Zhen the beginning and end of South's visit, with Zhen narrating in turn. In the secondary carriage, relying on the power of the visit to connect and influence, South finally achieved success. The reason why he did not immediately give Juan to him was out of fear of misinterpretation. A short while later, they entered the curtain and were intimate, still like a maiden. The next day, they thanked each other. At this point, the truth was finally revealed. South said, "You did not pick up the lost gold, so I also kept the whole jade waiting for you." From then on, Qin and Yue became one family, loving each other like siblings. South soon followed the example to apply for selection, became the Magistrate of Yangzhou, and took Juan with him. He was not ashamed to admit it, and often told his subordinates, "Who would have thought that even in the imperial list, there are beggars from the lowly fields?" The Annoyed Man said, "The man wearing a fur coat once said, 'Am I someone who picks up lost gold?'" However, it is easy for a high person to meet, but it is not easy for a beggar to meet. When faced with beautiful women, to rein in one's desires, this kind of effort is particularly difficult. Great is the Southern Palace! It is appropriate for the vast heavens to reward him with great virtue. I wish that all men in the world, regardless of wealth or poverty, should write the four characters "Blue Sky and White Sun" on the right side of their seats.

    21

    银雁
    　　
    　　江西某郡，有地师杜君香草，青鸟术最精。尝与富室儿李十九友善。其父故，杜为走山谷觅地，草履几穿。阅三载，始得一穴，沙水分明，良为吉壤。居邑之东山，距李宅四十余里，山故幽僻，树木葱笼，游者听樵斧丁丁，与岩寺午钟相问答；而西山居其前，俨如屏障，苍翠溢眸。杜得意非常，告李曰：“此福地也。令尊人古道，为乡里重，居此，谅无愧焉。愿公等更修德以培之，则子孙贵真莫及。”不日，杜为点穴，李兄弟扶榇窀穸事蒇。

    Explanation

    21

    Silver Geese

    In a certain county of Jiangxi, there was a geomancer named Du Xiangcao, who was highly skilled in the Qingniao technique. He was close friends with Li Shisao, a wealthy man. After Li's father passed away, Du set out into the mountains to find a suitable burial site. His straw sandals nearly wore through from the extensive travel. After three years, he finally found a site where the sand and water were clearly distinct, making it a truly auspicious place. The site was located in the eastern hills of the city, about forty miles from Li's residence. The mountains were naturally secluded and remote, with lush trees. Travelers could hear the chopping sounds of woodcutters, which echoed with the noon bell of a rock temple. The western hills stood in front of the site, like a natural barrier, with green and lush scenery filling the eyes. Du felt extremely pleased and told Li, "This is a blessed place. Your respected father's ancient virtues made him highly esteemed in the village. To be buried here, there is certainly no cause for regret. I hope you will further cultivate virtue to nurture it, then your descendants will be blessed and prosperous." Soon after, Du marked the site, and Li's brothers completed the burial arrangements.

    22

    杜往浙，应贵人聘，厚赆远行，一别两载。讵葬后，家道反不如前，迭亡人口。其兄李十八，夫妇亦偕亡，遗一娇女，名银雁；弥留时，殷殷以弱息相托，泣曰：“吾夫妇他无所恋，唯劳吾弟看顾银儿，则瞑目泉台下！”十九亦泣，受遗言。女年甫十四五，风致袅娜，不惯操作。其妇翁氏，黑心符也，时于稿砧前訾犹女懒惰；久之，遂信。垢面篷头，沦入赤脚。女始伏枕上泣，继对木主啼。妇怒施鞭挞。女奔告叔，反索其手足，而毒楚之。诸婢环泣，伏叩请代，不许。雪肤流血，一线仅延。妇怒犹未已。

    22

    Du went to Zhejiang, accepted the proposal of a nobleman, was heavily gifted for a long journey, and parted ways for two years. After the burial, the family's fortunes declined, and people died in succession. His brother, Li Shiba, and his wife also died together, leaving behind a beautiful daughter named Yin Yan. At the moment of death, he earnestly entrusted his weak offspring, crying, "My husband and I have no other attachments, only trouble my younger brother to take care of Yin'er, then I can close my eyes in peace in the netherworld!" Nineteen also cried and accepted the last words. The girl was just fourteen or fifteen, graceful and delicate, but not used to working. His mother-in-law, Black Heart Fu, often criticized the girl for being lazy in front of the family. After a long time, she came to believe it. The girl's face was dirty and her hair messy, and she fell into poverty. The girl started crying while lying on the bed, then she cried in front of the ancestral tablet. The mother-in-law angrily beat her. The girl ran to tell her uncle, but instead, she grabbed her hands and feet, and inflicted severe pain on her. The maids gathered around and cried, kneeling and begging to be punished instead, but she refused. Her white skin was bleeding, and only a thread of life remained. The mother-in-law's anger was not yet satisfied.

    23

    幸先陇邻庵老尼来，为翁氏讽受生经，急代缓颊，始释。明夕，妇欲礼佛，索温水盥手，女误以冷水进，大怒，欲笞其背。女惧，奔投门外溪水，见亡母冉冉自林中出，悲告女曰：“儿无苦，且随老尼遁空门，则生矣。”言已即杳。女正惨痛，忽闻门内喧嚷，翁氏自批颊，作亡母声詈曰：“狗贱根！何仇欲杀吾女？”十九奔入，知为亡嫂怒妇恶，代哀之，即唾其面，数责万端。急寻女，得于门外，审觅死状，携入。妇即抱女，哭失声；旋又自击，以木杵捣阴户，血淋淋流出穷外。禁止之，力猛于虎。

    Explanation

    23

    Fortunately, an elderly nun from a nearby temple arrived, reciting scriptures for the husband to ensure the daughter's rebirth. She urgently interceded to smooth things over, and the situation was finally resolved. The next night, the wife wished to worship Buddha and asked for warm water to wash her hands. The girl mistakenly brought cold water, which greatly angered the wife, who wanted to whip her back. Fearing for her life, the girl ran and jumped into the stream outside the door, where she saw her deceased mother slowly emerge from the forest. Her mother sadly told her, "You won't suffer; if you follow the old nun into the monastery, then you will be saved." After speaking, she disappeared immediately. The girl was deeply grieved. Suddenly, she heard commotion inside the door. The husband slapped his own cheeks, in the voice of the deceased mother, and scolded, "Filthy dog! What grudge do you have to want to kill my daughter?" The 19-year-old girl rushed in, knowing it was the angry and evil wife of the deceased sister-in-law. She spoke on her behalf, spat in her face, and counted and blamed countless things. Urgently searching for the girl, she found her outside the door, examined her condition, and carried her in. The wife immediately hugged the girl and cried out in grief. Then, she struck herself, with a wooden pestle, pounded her private parts, and blood flowed out profusely. The narrator stopped her with strength as fierce as a tiger.

    24

    一室大哗，邻舍登墙窥，鸡犬皆惊。老尼素与银母善，合掌曰：“善哉善哉！大娘何暴也？老身为证，嘱渠改过，善视银姑。”不许；“早为银姑觅佳婿。”不许；“寄养戚里家。”仍不许。尼戏曰：“无已，其将付老身携去为弟子乎？”妇闻即稽首拜，曰：“愿以掌珍，烦大师接引。”问女愿否？曰：“愿。”十九知不相能，亦听其出家。明日亲送之庵。拟即削云鬓，尼曰：“尚早。”瞑目，坐蒲团炊许，张目笑曰：“溪水何妨随石转，岭云更有出山时。”十九临去，谓女曰：“儿有所需，可暗嘱师父来取，毋轻蹈不测也。

    Explanation

    24

    The entire room was in uproar, neighbors climbed over the wall to peek, and even the chickens and dogs were frightened. The old nun, who had always been friendly with Silver Mother, clasped her hands and said, "Very good! Why are you so harsh, Madam? I, as a witness, will admonish him/her to reform and treat Silver Girl well." She did not agree; "Find a good husband for Silver Girl early." She still did not agree; "Place her in a relative's home." She still refused. The nun jokingly said, "If nothing else, will you entrust her to me to take her away as a disciple?" The woman heard and immediately prostrated herself in worship, saying, "I wish to entrust her to you, trouble the master to guide her." She asked if the girl agreed, and the girl said, "Yes." Nineteen knew they were not compatible and also allowed her to leave home. The next day, she personally sent her to the temple. She planned to shave her hair immediately, but the nun said, "It's still early." She closed her eyes and sat on a meditation cushion for a while, then opened her eyes and smiled, saying, "Why should the stream not turn with the stone, the mountain clouds will have their time to leave the mountain." Nineteen, on the verge of leaving, said to the girl, "If you need anything, you can secretly instruct the master to come and fetch, do not lightly step into danger."

    25

    ”女涕泣牵裾，尼大喝曰：“痴儿，既皈依三宝，尚作婴儿恋乳状耶？”急送十九出，掩庵扉。由是扫地焚香，钟鱼梵呗，渐授之以禅课焉。年余，十九益困顿，戚属咸云：“坐新阡不利。”有识者云：“右沙太耸，即利，亦只发女家。”明年，杜归，目睹李之情状，大惊，心亦疑殡宫风水。然昼则走山谷，夕则挑灯，比对书册，均无误，终不识致困之因。

    Explanation

    25

    The woman wept and clung to the robe, and the nun shouted loudly, "Foolish child, having already taken refuge in the Three Jewels, are you still acting like an infant clinging to its mother's breast?" She quickly sent the nineteen-year-old out and closed the temple door. From then on, she swept the floor, burned incense, rang the bell, and chanted the scriptures, gradually teaching her the Chan lessons. After a year, the nineteen-year-old became increasingly troubled. Relatives all said, "It's inauspicious to be buried in the new grave." Those with knowledge said, "The right sand is too high; even if it's auspicious, it only benefits the woman's family." The next year, Du returned, seeing Li's condition, he was greatly shocked, and he also suspected the geomantic layout of the burial site. However, during the day he would wander in the mountains, and at night he would light the lamp and compare books, all without error, yet he never understood the cause of the trouble.

    26

    一夕，假归家宿，夜梦天女至，烟鬟雾鬓，仪态万千，告杜曰：“汝亦知李墓不吉，所由来乎？吾山神也，特以诗句告汝，诗云：‘千里来龙结一匏，左根右叶长根苗；天生福人住福地，无愧唯有西山樵’。”杜心欲咨白，而天女遽作霹雳乘云去。杜惊寤，急往西山，寻一泛常穴，为李迁葬。诡云“前穴地脉，为山风吹破，不足宝也。”暇则裹粮入西山，物色樵竖，匝月无一遇。一日暴雨至，视岭前有衡茅数椽，急奔避。一婆娑老妪，服出应客。堂上悬桐棺七尺，穗帐凄然，云：“伊稿砧，亡周七年。”问：“有哲嗣否？”云：“仅一，杜姓，佛奴其名，盖渠父梦佛而生者。因贫，习樵采，日在东山云深处。”言已嘘。向外翘首，云：“顷将遭雨，恐归来又似水淋鸡。”旋入，以山茶炊饼饷杜，餐饮颇甘。

    Explanation

    26

    One night, Du took leave to return home and spend the night there. That night, he dreamed of a celestial maiden arriving, her hair swirling like smoke and mist, with a myriad of graceful postures and expressions. She told Du, "Do you also know that Li's tomb is inauspicious? Where does this come from? I am a mountain deity, and I specially inform you through poetry. The poem goes: 'A thousand miles of winding mountains form a gourd, with roots on the left and leaves on the right, growing into branches; Heaven-born fortunate people live in blessed places, and only the woodcutter of the Western Mountain is without shame.'" Du wanted to consult and ask her, but the maiden suddenly transformed into thunder and rode away on the clouds. Du woke up in shock and rushed to the Western Mountain to find an ordinary grave site for Li's reburial. He deceptively said, "The previous grave site's geomantic energy was broken by the mountain wind, so it is not valuable." When free, he packed food and went into the Western Mountain, searching for the woodcutter. He searched for a month without meeting anyone. One day, heavy rain came, and he saw several thatched huts at the foot of the hill, so he hurried to take refuge. An elderly woman with flowing garments came out to greet him. In the hall, a seven-foot paulownia coffin hung, and a tasseled curtain looked mournful. She said, "My husband, the millstone, has been dead for seven years." Du asked, "Do you have a wise successor?" She replied, "Only one, named Du, called 'Buddha Slave,' because his father dreamed of a Buddha and gave birth to him. Due to poverty, he learned to cut wood and gather firewood, working in the deep clouds of the Eastern Mountain." After speaking, she sighed. Looking out, she said, "Just now, I was about to get rained on, fearing I would return looking like a chicken drenched in water." She quickly went inside and served Du with tea and steamed buns, and the meal was quite delicious.

    27

    须臾，一少年荷樵冒雨归，眉目端好，气宇不凡，见客支揖如儒者。杜知是佛奴，自陈同姓；佛奴入，与母言。少顷，又出，与为礼，如见长上。杜喜，与闲话，辞均闲雅，无俚语。告妪曰：“文郎不俗，何不令读书识字？”妪曰：“儿幼曾就村馆，渠父既殁，未亡人又衰迈，全赖是儿斧柯供菽水。”问佛奴年齿，则云十七。是夕，即就地藉席止杜宿。晨起，出腰金二两，酬茶果费。妪笑曰：“母子虽贫，而非卖茶果者，矧同宗者耶！”坚却不受。杜知不可强。后累过其庐，礼貌均不衰。一日，见佛奴获双雉归，烹而登盘，味极鲜美。

    Explanation

    27

    In a moment, a young man carrying firewood returned home in the rain. He had good-looking eyebrows and eyes, an unusual bearing, and greeted the guest with a respectful bow, like a scholar. Du recognized him as Foni and learned that they shared the same surname. Foni entered and spoke with his mother. A short while later, he came out again and performed a ritual, showing respect as if seeing an elder. Du was pleased and engaged in casual conversation with him, whose speech was equally leisurely and elegant, without any vulgar language. Du told the old woman, "The young man is not vulgar. Why not let him read and learn characters?" The old woman replied, "The child was once in a village school. His father has already died, and the widow is also old and feeble. We completely depend on this son to support us." When Du asked Foni his age, Foni said he was seventeen. That night, Foni arranged for Du to sleep on a mat. In the morning, Du took out two taels of gold from his waist to pay for the tea and fruit. The old woman laughed and said, "Though we are poor, we are not selling tea and fruit. Especially since we are of the same clan!" She firmly refused to accept the payment. Du knew it could not be forced. Later, he often passed by their thatched cottage, and their manners remained unchanged. One day, he saw Foni catch two pheasants and return. He cooked them and placed them on a tray, and the taste was extremely delicious.

    28

    盘有余，收入供母；及偷瞰佛奴，则仍咽粗粝。杜大敬重，告妪，拟为佛奴觅佳偶。妪喜曰：“儿年已冠，得宗长作伐，大好事。但一贫如洗，谁肯为娇女嫁樵人子耶？”问：“渠父可有葬地？”曰：“野葬耳。免入漏泽国足矣，尚敢卜牛眠欤？”曰：“不难，仆有吉壤奉赠，他日贵显，幸无忘指引人。”妪敬谢称善。杜向李十九索前地，云：“有远族孤寡，请以所弃者与之，需值当不吝偿”。李慨然不吝。杜欲立券，李本拟以百金酬杜，至是遂兑立百金券，杜持往与妪诹吉。佛奴聚众樵，舁父柩，诣葬穴。杜命五尺，无移旧穴，而深倍之。

    Explanation

    28

    When there is surplus, it is put away to support the mother; but when peeking at Buddha's servant, he still eats coarse food. Du Da respects greatly and informs the old woman, planning to find a good match for Buddha's servant. The old woman is happy and says, "The son is already of age, and getting the clan elder to arrange the match is a great thing. But he is as poor as washing clothes, who would be willing to marry their beautiful daughter to a son of a woodcutter?" She asks, "Does the father have a burial ground?" The old woman replies, "Buried in the wild. Just to avoid being buried in the public cemetery is enough, how dare one predict a good resting place for cattle?" She says, "It is not difficult, I have a good piece of land to give. In the future, if you become prominent, please do not forget the person who guided you." The old woman respectfully thanks and praises it. Du asks Li Shíjiǔ for the previous land, saying, "There are distant relatives who are widows and orphans, please give them the abandoned land, the value should not be hesitated to pay." Li generously does not hesitate. Du wants to make a contract, and Li originally intended to pay Du with a hundred gold coins, so he exchanges and establishes a contract for a hundred gold coins. Du takes it to the old woman to choose a good day. Buddha's servant gathers the crowd to chop wood, carries the father's coffin, and goes to the burial site. Du orders five feet, without moving the old grave, and doubles the depth.

    29

    甫掘尺许，得一物，非土非石，状类鼋鼍，背有篆文曰：“识者杜，葬者杜，宜子孙，贵且富。鲜德之家，莫妄觑。”葬已，杜又远行。佛奴仍习樵，每晨过父墓，辄见蒸腾如釜上气。瞬届严寒，佛奴正痴望，忽白气接冻云，缕缕然，漠漠然，落落然，霎时雨雪大至，衣尽沾濡。知岭下有尼庵可避，急趋叩门，适老尼打包出门去，遗银雁独居绣佛幡。

    Explanation

    29

    Just as he had dug a few inches, he found an object that was neither earth nor stone, resembling a turtle or alligator. On its back were inscriptions in ancient script that read: "Those who recognize it are Du, those who bury it are Du, it is auspicious for descendants, bringing both nobility and wealth. Do not covet this if you lack virtue." After the burial, Du went on a distant journey. Buddha's servant continued to work as a woodcutter, passing by his father's grave every morning and seeing a rising vapor like steam from a pot. Suddenly, the severe cold arrived, and Buddha's servant was gazing in wonder when suddenly white vapor met frozen clouds, gradually and diffusely, and in an instant, heavy rain and snow fell, soaking his clothes. Knowing there was a nunnery in the valley where one could take shelter, he hurriedly ran to knock on the door. Just as an old nun was packing to leave, she left behind a silver swan and an embroidered Buddhist banner.

    30

    开门放入，见其寒战噤栗，怜之，引投灶下，燃火烘湿衣，以师之布衲与己之紫布裤与之换，更炊豆粥与餐，战始已。天霁衣干，佛奴欲辞去，一转瞬，则他衣俱在，而己之布裤竟乌有，穷觅不见。女恐师回，催促且去，嘱乘间寄裤来，慎勿寓师目。其母嗔其归晏，具告所以。妪心德女，视紫衣果为女子衷衣，疑有染，叱责之，佛奴力白其无。明日，妪亲送裤与女，而老尼已归，见而穷诘之，大怒云：“清静道场，淫婢何得污佛地！”立命走出。妪同女跪求，不许。女对佛自誓，尼冷笑曰：“佛远在大西天，不似社稷神，管尔牙疼咒也。

    Explanation

    30

    Opening the door, she let him in and saw him shivering uncontrollably from the cold. Feeling pity for him, she led him to the stove, lit a fire to dry his wet clothes, and gave him the teacher's patched robe and her own purple pants to wear. She then cooked a bean porridge for him to eat, and his shivering finally subsided. The sky cleared, and his clothes dried. The Buddha's servant wanted to leave, but in a moment, he found that all his other clothes were there, except for his own purple pants, which he had lost and could not find. The girl was afraid the teacher would return, so she urged him to leave quickly and instructed him to bring the pants back later, being careful not to let the teacher see them. Her mother scolded her for returning late and told her the whole story. The old woman felt grateful to the girl and saw the purple clothes as indeed a girl's undergarment, suspecting it was stained. She scolded the girl, but the Buddha's servant strongly defended herself, saying it wasn't true. The next day, the old woman personally delivered the pants to the girl, but the old nun had already returned. Upon seeing the pants, she questioned the girl harshly and flew into a rage, saying, "A pure place of worship, how can a lewd maid defile the Buddha's ground!" She immediately ordered the girl to leave. The old woman and the girl knelt and begged, but she refused. The girl took an oath before the Buddha, and the nun laughed coldly and said, "Buddha is far away in the Western Paradise, unlike the gods of the state who can cure your toothache."

    31

    ”女愤欲自缢，解带挂庭树，妪趋救而尼犹怒，妪亦怨曰：“老秃厮，乃徒以慈悲获罪责，然则定逐伊何处去？”尼曰：“听自便耳！”妪知女不愿回俗家，曰：“曷随老身去？”女犹踌躇，尼遽抚掌，曰：“妙哉妙哉！速去速去！”立即驱出，掩双扉。适杜正归来看妪，见妪携女至，惊询何来，详告所以。女伏地涕泣呼杜叔，杜喜曰：“前云为佛郎作冰者，即此女耳！此中有天缘，幸勿错过！”约略告十九，即慨分游橐，代谋花烛俾合卺。小夫妇极伉俪，事母又至孝。妪对女流涕曰：“吾母子食贫惯，未免苦新妇。”女笑曰：“儿昔遭婶苦虐，如活地狱；后入尼庵，已若安乐窝。

    Explanation

    31

    A woman, furious and intent on hanging herself, untied her sash and hung it on a tree in the courtyard. The old woman rushed to save her, but the nun remained angry. The old woman also complained, saying, "Old bald fellow, you're only being punished for your compassion. Then where exactly are you going to send her?" The nun replied, "Let her go her own way!" Knowing the woman did not wish to return to her former life, the old woman suggested, "Why not come with me?" The woman hesitated, and the nun suddenly clapped her hands, exclaiming, "Wonderful, wonderful! Go quickly, go quickly!" She immediately drove her out and closed the double doors. Just as Du was returning to see the old woman, he saw her with the woman, and was surprised, asking where she came from. She explained in detail why she was there. The woman fell to the ground, wept, and called out to Uncle Du. Du was pleased and said, "The one who said she would make ice for the Buddha, that is this woman! There is a heavenly connection here, so don't miss it!" He roughly told her about nineteen, and immediately generously divided his travel bag, arranging for a marriage and bringing them together. The young couple were extremely devoted to each other and were also very filial to their mother. The old woman wept and said to the woman, "My mother and I are used to living in poverty, so we might make your life difficult." The woman smiled and said, "I used to suffer from my aunt's cruel treatment, as if I were in hell; later, when I entered the nunnery, it was like a place of peace and comfort."

    32

    顷得阿母与郎怜爱，更居天堂最上层。”妪为之解颐。每牧豕，分郎苦，妪不忍，女曰：“儿自乐为之。昔之牧豕，迫威虐；今之牧豕，由心愿，何害？”杜一日携白镪二锭，来告妪曰：“母今新娶妇，添食指矣。明年秫价必昂，请假此预收秫，俾佛郎渐学权子母。”三却而后受。是夕，银雁牧豕回，见妪以银示佛奴，曰：“是区区者，亦犹之铜铁耳，奈何有则生，无则死，不令人短气耶！”女索视掷案上，曰：“是何足贵！儿牧豕走涧曲，见水底累累者皆是也。明当怀数枚归而奉母。”妪笑曰：“痴妮子！误以鹅卵石为银耶？”明日，女果怀数枚至，外虽花绣黝然，就石磨砻，光可鉴影。大惊喜。

    Explanation

    32

    Recently, the child has gained the love and affection of the grandmother and the young master, now living in the highest level of heaven. The grandmother smiled in relief. Whenever she herded pigs, she shared the young master's hardships, but the grandmother could not bear it. The girl said, "I am happy to do it myself. In the past, herding pigs was under the pressure of tyranny; now, herding pigs is by one's own will, what harm is there?" One day, Du carried two ingots of silver, came to tell the grandmother, saying, "Mother has recently taken a new wife, added another mouth to feed. Next year, the price of millet will surely rise. Please lend these to pre-purchase millet, so that Buddha Lang can gradually learn the art of usury." The grandmother refused three times before accepting. That evening, when Silver Goose returned from herding pigs, she saw the grandmother showing the silver to Buddha Lang, saying, "These small things are also like copper and iron. Why does it mean life when you have it and death when you don't, making one feel short of breath?" The girl took a look and threw them on the table, saying, "What is there to value so highly! I herded pigs in the winding stream and saw that the bottom was full of them. Tomorrow, I will bring a few back to give to my mother." The grandmother laughed and said, "Foolish girl! Did you mistake pebbles for silver?" The next day, the girl indeed brought a few back. Though they were dark and embroidered on the outside, when ground on a stone, they shone brightly enough to reflect a shadow. Greatly surprised.

    33

    问多寡，云：“遍涧底都是。”佛奴趋诣村市，询于人，曰：“此古人窖藏物也。”归与母计。晨起，随女之涧曲，见流水潺潺，下皆卵石，唯自女手掬起，则立成朱提。始犹以布袋运，继因误堕一锭，牧竖拾之，笑问：“母子劬劳，大辛苦，运蠢物何用？”然一入牧竖手，则仍化为石。由是胆大壮，居然筐载而肩荷之。匝月往返，始竭。屋角堆满无寸隙，佛奴掘深窖藏之。统计约有二十余万。会入城，买甲马酬藏神。晤杜，即邀致，详告之，且分赠。杜不受，急代购郭市膏腴，建第宅，阡陌楼台，奴婢车马，成大家矣。

    Explanation

    33

    When they inquired about the quantity, the villagers replied, "It's all over the bottom of the stream." Foni hurried to the village market to ask people, and they said, "This must be an ancient treasure buried by someone." Upon returning, he discussed it with his mother. At dawn, they followed the girl to the winding stream, where they saw the water flowing gently, with pebbles at the bottom. However, when the girl picked up the pebbles, they instantly turned into gold. At first, they used cloth bags to carry the gold, but later, when a piece accidentally dropped and was picked up by a shepherd boy, he laughed and asked, "Your mother and son are working so hard, what use is there in transporting such a clumsy thing?" Indeed, as soon as the gold entered the shepherd boy's hand, it immediately turned back into stone. Thus, they became bolder and stronger, loading the gold into baskets and carrying it on their shoulders. After a month of back-and-forth trips, they finally exhausted the supply. The corners of their house were piled up with no inch of space left, so Foni dug a deep pit to hide the gold. They estimated that there were around two hundred thousand pieces. When they entered the city, they bought a horse to thank the deity of the treasure. They met Du and invited him over, telling him in detail about the treasure and distributing some as gifts. Du did not accept, so they quickly bought fertile land in the market, built a mansion, with fields, terraces, towers, and pavilions, and servants and carriages, becoming a wealthy and influential family.

    34

    明年，女又孪生双子，曰鸿曰鸾，均颖慧，能读，总角即游庠序。佛奴亦纳粟为员外郎，为太母请封诰。每称觞介眉寿，太母辄云：“儿孙虽千百年，不可忘宗人香草先主德。”久之，延于家，事之若伯叔云。会清明，夫妻佩绋扫父墓，干仆夹道，婢妾如云。忽一穷汉号哭奔至，崩角乞援，倏一保正执牛鞭来擒捉。佛奴怜讯之，云：“此小窃屡犯者，是必断其胫股，员外郎莫袒庇。”女闻声，牵车帘，视其人，非他，李十九也。问何至此，自云家业尽倾，恶妇随奴子遁，席卷烬馀，孑然一身，时就野庙宿，实未曾作穿窬也。

    Explanation

    34

    The following year, the woman gave birth to twins, named Hong and Luan, both of whom were intelligent and could read. They attended school as children. The husband, named Fohu, also bought the title of an official and requested an official title for his mother-in-law. Whenever they lifted the cup to wish for a long life, the mother-in-law would say, "Even if my descendants live for a thousand years, they must not forget the virtues of our ancestor, the noble person who loved fragrant grass." She was treated with respect, as if she were an uncle or aunt.

    On Qingming Festival, the couple visited their father's tomb while wearing mourning clothes. Servants lined up on both sides of the road, and maids and concubines were like clouds. Suddenly, a poor man ran to them crying, prostrated himself, and begged for help. A village headman suddenly came with a whip to catch him. Fohu showed pity and asked him, saying, "This thief has committed crimes before, and he must have his legs cut off. Do not protect him, official." The wife heard the sound, pulled the curtain of the carriage, and saw the man. It was none other than Li Shijiu. She asked him why he was there, and he explained that all his property had been lost, his bad wife had run away with the servant, and he had swept away all the remains, leaving him alone and destitute. Sometimes, he stayed at a temple in the wild. He truly had never committed theft.

    35

    言已大哭，女亦悲涕。佛奴笑遣里正去，携归，事以泰山礼，李局促不敢当；及杜香草出与话旧，更惭，坚欲辞去。遂赠以婢，且与数百金，俾理旧业焉。明年，二子场乡捷，太母正八十寿，宾客满堂祝千秋。忽庵内小尼云：“奉师命，坚请银夫人随喜。”且以旧犊鼻缄寄，云：“此杜郎旧物也。”佛奴欲辞却，女不可，珠翠肩舆往。至则老尼已沐浴更衣，瞑目将就涅盘。

    Explanation

    35

    After she had spoken, she wept bitterly, and the girl also shed tears. Buddha Slave laughed and sent the village headman away, then took her home, treating her with the grand rites of Mount Tai. Li felt awkward and dared not accept; when Du Xiangcao came out and they talked about old times, he felt even more ashamed and firmly wanted to leave. He then gave her a maid and several hundred gold pieces, to help her manage her old business. The following year, his two sons passed the provincial exams, and the great mother was celebrating her eightieth birthday, with guests filling the hall to wish her a thousand autumns of life. Suddenly, a young nun from the temple said, "Following the master's command, we firmly invite Lady Silver to join in the joy." She also sent an old sleeve as a token, saying, "This is an old possession of Du Lang." Buddha Slave wanted to decline, but the girl insisted, so they went in a carriage adorned with jewels and pearls. When they arrived, the old nun had already bathed and changed her clothes, closing her eyes, about to enter nirvana.

    36

    女哀哀唤苏，尼目复启，笑曰：“儿果荣贵，则他日遣逐，不以老比丘为太过耶？”女泣曰：“法师生死人而肉白骨，何敢忘德！”曰：“不足言德，唯可以对令先慈于地下耳。”言已圆寂。女出资为尼新院宇，置沃产，建藏骨浮图于庵侧，其位为先陇之艮方，状尖耸，如剑戟。工竣，杜视之，笑曰：“后世子孙中，更当得一武鼎甲”，已而果验。此余在滋阳，听浙人孙君子任所述者。孙君诚厚长者，其言谅非虚构。懊侬氏曰：马鬣牛眠，贤豪蜕骨，自古山灵面目，何尝轻易示人也。唯涧底累累，百万朱提，专留与贤孝之妇，如我银娘者。则铜臭之神，又何尝毫无知识，专与牧竖子为伍哉！常见席丰履厚之家，其德其才，绝不称是，盖其祖德宗功，必有大过人处。噫！此其所以为神也！

    Explanation

    36

    A woman called out in sorrow, trying to wake someone up. The nun's eyes reopened, and she smiled, saying, "If you truly achieve honor and wealth, then one day you will be dismissed, and would it not be too much to dismiss an old monk?" The woman wept and said, "The master brought the dead back to life and fleshed out the bones; how dare I forget your kindness!" The nun replied, "It is not necessary to speak of kindness; only you can tell your mother in the afterlife." After speaking these words, the nun passed away peacefully. The woman contributed funds for the construction of a new temple for the nun, established a fertile estate, and built a stupa for the storage of bones next to the hermitage. The stupa was located at the northeast corner of the ancestral grave, with a sharp and rising shape like a sword or halberd. Upon completion of the work, the nun looked at it and smiled, saying, "In future generations, there will surely be a military champion." And indeed, this prediction came true. This is what I heard from Mr. Sun Zirun, a native of Zhejiang, while I was in Zizheng. Mr. Sun is a sincere and generous elder, and his words are surely not fabricated. Agnostic says: The resting place of the wise and virtuous, the bones of the heroic and noble, the face of the mountain spirits from ancient times—how could they ever show themselves easily to people? Only at the bottom of the ravine, countless treasures, reserved specifically for virtuous and filial women, like my silver-haired maiden. The god of wealth, how could he be completely ignorant and associate only with shepherds and cowherds? I often see families that are wealthy and well-established, whose virtue and talent do not match their wealth and status. It is likely that their ancestors had great virtues and achievements. Ah! This is why they are considered gods!

    37

    王大姑
    　　
    　　峄阳西南，邻丰沛诸境，台儿庄当其冲。庄有巨族王氏，所居比栉。王叟某，老夫妇生子女各一。子名懋修，廪膳生，常远就臬比，坐博菽水，攻举业。女名大姑，貌楚楚，性敏慧，幼读曹娥庞娥诸列传，未尝不掩卷而泣也。适某生，素患瘵，结缡甫半年，女三割臂上肉，不能救其死；思以身殉，又恐伤亲心。然夫家又赤贫，且无人。女兄懋修遂迎之归，谓女曰：“兄不克昕夕侍二老，即烦吾妹代兄职。”女曰：“诺。”定省温清之善，直钗而弁也。族无遐迩皆贤之。是年夏，捻贼将至，风鹤宵警，鬼车夜号。叟本王氏族长，呼众远迁，各户咸营营于辎重。

    Explanation

    37

    Wang Dagu

    In the southwest of Yizhou, near the regions of Feng and Pei, lies Tai'erzhuang, strategically positioned at a crossroads. The village is home to a prominent family, the Wangs, whose residences are neatly arranged. An elderly couple, the Wangs, had one son and one daughter. The son, named Maoxiu, was a government-funded student who often traveled far to visit officials, enjoying a simple life while studying for the imperial examinations. The daughter, named Dagu, was graceful and intelligent, and from a young age, she read biographies of filial figures like Cao E and Pang E, often moved to tears by their stories. She married a man who was already ill; after being married for just half a year, she cut flesh from her arm three times in an attempt to save him, but he died. She considered taking her own life to join him, fearing it would hurt her parents. However, her husband's family was extremely poor and lacked support. Her brother, Maoxiu, then invited her to return home and said to her, "I cannot serve our parents in the morning and evening, so I ask my sister to take on my duties." Dagu replied, "I will." She performed the duties of filial piety, wearing a hairpin and a hat to care for her parents. The entire clan admired her. That summer, the Nian rebels were approaching, causing panic and unrest. The old man, the head of the Wang clan, called the people to move far away, and each household was busy with the transport of goods and supplies.

    38

    女进谋曰：“仓猝出奔，全赖牛车代步，载人行犹速，兼物行则滞，遇贼必因物丧命，不遇贼亦必为宵小觊觎。重物轻人，诚非良算。计不若掘地藏物，单车载人，可望出虎口，而登乐土耳。”众思其言良善，即如所谋，举族以行。老稚约百余口，甫出庄十里，猝遇贼于途，遍搜括无携带，然服饰面目，均非藜藿者流。大呼曰：“狡哉伧也！家资匿何所？不自首献者，斩无赦！”众皆觳觫，面色死灰，崩角无一语。女含笑下车，裣衽而前曰：“大王无怒，若皆农家子，非善于语言者。我即彼族司管钥人也，黄白岂无，窖藏诚有，彼大树葱笼，庐舍翳如者，是所居耳。如从我往，一一指示，十万金咄嗟办。

    Explanation

    38

    The woman proposed a plan: "In our hasty flight, we must rely entirely on ox carts for transportation. Carrying people allows us to move faster, but carrying objects slows us down. If we encounter bandits, we will surely lose our lives due to the objects; if not met by bandits, it will surely be coveted by petty thieves. Prioritizing objects over people, truly not a good strategy. It is better to dig a hole to hide the objects and use a single cart to carry people. One can hope to escape the jaws of the tiger and reach a place of joy." The crowd thought her words were good and acted as she proposed, and the whole family set out. About a hundred elderly and young, just ten miles out of the village, they suddenly met bandits on the road. The bandits searched everywhere and found no belongings, but their clothing and appearance were not that of commoners. They shouted loudly, "Cunning and vulgar indeed! Where are your assets hidden? Those who do not voluntarily surrender will be executed without pardon!" Everyone trembled, their faces ashen, and stood in silence without a word. The woman smiled, got off the cart, and stepped forward saying, "Do not be angry, Your Majesty. They are all farmers, not skilled in speech. I am the one who manages the keys for that group. There is gold and silver, and there is indeed a hidden treasure. That place with tall trees, covered houses, is where we live. If you follow me there, I can show you everything, and I can provide you with ten thousand gold coins in no time."

    39

    否则骈死荒郊，于大王毫无禅益。”捻大喜，赏其慧美，信其恳笃，乃舍众而随女以行。女慨然导，略反顾，以目示众，令遁，众始免脱焉。从行里许，至一极大村，诡云己宅，内外阒如，破键入，延贼坐厅事。女拾地下蒲葵扇，且摇且语曰：“大王等长途跋涉，马足奔驰，饥渴甚矣。且少憩纳凉，待我诣内煮茗，略尽东道谊。然而具畚锸，先掘我家，再掘他家也。”贼笑颔之。以为茕茕弱息，固已瓮鳖釜鱼，尚何外逸？解衣裸体，歌啸纵横。久之，日将堕崦嵫，瀹茗人仍不出，奔内搜阅，贼大骇，盖女已挂梁上，体冰而僵矣。贼恶其诳己，思淫其尸。甫解下，一贼大呼倒地，视脑后若有锥击，顷刻毙。

    Explanation

    39

    Otherwise, they would die in pairs in the wilderness, bringing no benefit to Your Majesty." The bandits were greatly pleased, rewarded her for her wisdom and beauty, and trusted her sincerity and earnestness. They left the others and followed the girl. The girl led them resolutely, glancing back briefly, and with a glance, she signaled the others to escape, and they finally escaped. They followed for about a mile, arriving at a very large village. She deceived them by claiming it was her home. Inside and out, it was empty. They broke open the door and entered, inviting the bandits to sit in the hall. The girl picked up a fan from the ground, fanning and speaking, "Your Majesty and your men have traveled long distances, with horses galloping. You must be very hungry and thirsty. Please rest and cool off for a while. Wait while I go inside to make tea, to fulfill the host's duty. However, prepare shovels and picks, first dig up my house, then yours." The bandits laughed and nodded. They thought she was a helpless and weak young woman, already trapped like fish in a pot or turtles in a jar, how could she possibly escape? They undressed and sang and shouted wildly. After a while, the sun was setting. The person making tea still did not come out. They rushed inside to search, and the bandits were greatly startled, for the girl had already hung herself from the beam, her body was cold and stiff. The bandits were angry that she had deceived them and planned to rape her corpse. Just as they were about to, one bandit cried out and fell to the ground. They saw that there was a spike-like object hitting the back of his head, and he died instantly.

    40

    虎狼之徒，亦知贞烈不可犯，罗拜而去。女兄懋修，时正解馆归，途闻台儿庄有王姓女子舍身全亲族事，生大哭，曰：“必我妹也！”至避难处，则家人已舁女尸回，亲与族围之哭。哀讯所以，始悉始末。生枕股大恸，曰：“苦吾妹矣！”既而跃起，大笑曰：“吾有妹矣！舍一己命，全二老命，且全合族命，须眉已难，矧巾帼乎？无怪当日读《烈女传》，泪随声堕，盖生有至性也。节且孝，烈且智，舍吾妹，谁能兼之？呜呼！”懊侬氏曰：以王氏合族百余人，猝遇贼氛，何不一战毕命？其所以束手待毙者，视贼如虎狼耳。而大姑视之，直犬彘不若矣。姑之节也可哭，孝也可敬，烈也可悲，智也可喜，贼之痴也可笑。

    Explanation

    40

    The bandits, like tigers and wolves, also knew that the woman's chastity and loyalty were inviolable, and they bowed in a circle and left. The elder sister, Maoxiu, was just returning from her teaching post when she heard the news that a woman surnamed Wang in Taierzhuang had sacrificed herself to save her entire family. Her brother, upon hearing this, wept bitterly and said, "It must be my sister!" When he arrived at the place where his sister had taken refuge, he found that the family had already brought her body back, and the relatives and family members were gathered around her, weeping. Upon learning the details of the event, he wept bitterly while leaning on his knees and said, "How unfortunate for my sister!" Then he stood up and laughed, saying, "I have a sister! She sacrificed her own life to save her parents and the entire family. Even men would find it difficult to do such a thing, let alone a woman. No wonder when reading the Biographies of Loyal Women, one's tears would fall as one reads, because it is a natural and genuine response. She is both filial and chaste, both brave and wise. Who else could possess all these virtues? Alas!" The commentator, Anong, said, "With the Wang family's hundred or so members suddenly encountering bandits, why didn't they fight to the death? Their decision to surrender and await death was because they viewed the bandits as tigers and wolves. Yet, the aunt treated them as if they were even less than dogs and pigs. Her chastity is worthy of tears, her filial piety is worthy of respect, her bravery is worthy of sorrow, and her wisdom is worthy of joy. The bandits' foolishness is laughable."

    41

    雅赚
    　　
    　　郑板桥先生，书法钟王，参以米蔡，转似篆隶；画则得所南瓮家法，更参以徐青藤老人，挥洒雄杰之致，便卓然大家。为秀才时，三至邗江，售书卖画，无识者，落拓可怜。复举于乡，旋登甲榜，声名大震。再至邗江，则争索先生墨妙者，户外履常满。先生固寒士，至是益盛自宝重，非重价，不与索。沈凡民先生代镌小印文，曰“二十年前旧板桥，”志愤也。时江西张真人入觐回，道出邗江，商人争媚之，欲得先生书联献真人。江西定做大笺纸，长丈余，阔六尺余，乃可一不可再者，使人婉求先生书，且请撰句。问需值，曰：“一千金。”来者允五百。

    Explanation

    41

    The Artful Deception
    　　
    　　Mr. Zheng Banqiao, a master of calligraphy, was influenced by the styles of Zhong Yao and Wang Xizhi, as well as Mi Fu and Cai Xiang, and his work resembled seal and clerical scripts. In painting, he mastered the techniques of the South Well School and further incorporated elements of Xu Gu's style, resulting in a powerful and heroic style that made him a distinguished master. As a scholar, he traveled to Yangzhou three times to sell his calligraphy and paintings but was unrecognized and lived a poor and pitiful life. After passing the provincial and metropolitan examinations and gaining widespread fame, he returned to Yangzhou, where people competed to buy his calligraphy and paintings, and his home was often crowded with visitors. Originally a poor scholar, he became more self-respecting and only sold his works for high prices. Shen Fanmin engraved a small seal for him, inscribed with "the old Zheng Banqiao from twenty years ago," to express his frustration. At the time, Zhang Zhenren, a Taoist master from Jiangxi, returned from an audience with the emperor, passing through Yangzhou, and local merchants competed to flatter him by seeking Zheng Banqiao's calligraphy couplets to present to him. The merchants in Jiangxi prepared large sheets of paper, measuring more than one zhang in length and more than six chi in width, which could only be used once, and sent a messenger to politely ask Zheng Banqiao to write on them and compose couplets. The messenger asked for the price, and Zheng Banqiao initially asked for one thousand gold pieces, but the messenger agreed to pay five hundred.

    42

    先生欣然，奋笔直扫，顷成上联，云：“龙虎山中真宰相，”求书次联，笑曰：“言明一千金，尔只与五百，我亦仅与其半。”其人往告商，不得已，如数与之。即书次联，曰：“麒麟阁上活神仙。”人人赞叹，工妙绝伦。其时，商家因盐政都转，咸重先生，遂争求先生书画，或联，或幅，或，或斗方，以为荣。各商皆得之，唯商人某甲，出身微贱，赋性尤鄙，先生恶之，虽重值，誓不允所请。某甲自顾厅事，无先生尺楮零缣，私衷羞恧，百计求之，终不得。先生性好游。一日，携短僮，负诗囊，信步出东郭，渐至无人踪。视乱坟丛葬间，隐隐有屋角，微露炊烟，花柳参差。笑曰：“岂此间有隐君子耶？”甫逾岭，而坟益多，径益窄。

    Explanation

    42

    The teacher was delighted and wrote with great vigor, quickly composing the upper couplet: "True Prime Minister in the Dragon and Tiger Mountains." When asked to write the lower couplet, he smiled and said, "I said it would cost a thousand gold coins, but you only gave five hundred, so I will only write half of it." The person went to inform the merchant, who, reluctantly, paid the full amount. The teacher then wrote the lower couplet: "Living Immortal on the Qilin Pavilion." Everyone praised it, saying it was skillfully and marvelously crafted. At that time, because of the merchant's position as the Salt Commissioner, all merchants highly regarded the teacher and competed to seek his calligraphy and paintings, whether it was couplets, scrolls, or square pieces, considering it an honor. All merchants obtained them, except for a certain merchant named Mr. Jia, who came from humble origins and had a particularly low character. The teacher disliked him and, even though he offered a high price, firmly refused his request. Mr. Jia looked around his hall, finding no piece of paper or silk with the teacher's writing, feeling ashamed and embarrassed. He tried many ways to obtain it but ultimately failed. The teacher loved to travel. One day, he took a short servant boy, carrying a bag of poetry, and walked leisurely out of the eastern suburb. Gradually, he reached a place where there were no traces of people. He saw a chaotic burial ground, with a faint corner of a house and a slight wisp of smoke visible among the graves, and flowers and willows scattered about. He smiled and said, "Could there be a hidden gentleman here?" As soon as he crossed the hill, the tombs became more numerous, and the path became narrower.

    43

    再一回头，则有小村落在焉。茅屋数椽，制绝精雅，四无邻舍，又无墙垣；小桥通溪，即至门首。白板上一联云：“逃出刘伶禅外住，喜向苏髯腹内居，”上有小额，云：“怪叟行窝。”进门，又得一重门，联云：“月白风清，此处更容谁卜宅；麟阴焰聚，平生喜与鬼为邻。”额云：“富儿绝迹。”庭中笼鸟盆鱼，与花药相掩映。新种芭蕉，才有掌大；乍添杨柳，却比人高。朝南有室两楹，洒扫无纤尘，内置几一，案一，椅四，杌二，木榻藤枕书橱各一，琴剑竹搁又各一。案上笔砚纸墨，乌丝尺，水中丞，皆备。壁上悬青藤老人补天图，女娲氏螺髻高颡，仰视炉鼎中，气冉冉入空际，生气勃发，的为真迹。

    Explanation

    43

    Turning one's head again, there is a small village in sight. A thatched cottage with a few rooms, its design is extremely elegant and refined, with no neighboring houses around and no walls or fences; a small bridge leads to a stream, right to the entrance. On a white board, a couplet reads: "Escaping from Liu Ling's hermitage to live, delighted to reside within Su Shi's intellectual sphere," above which is a small sign reading: "The Dwelling of the Eccentric Old Man." Upon entering, another gate is found, with a couplet reading: "The moon is bright and the wind is clear, who else can find a place to settle here; the spiritual energy gathers, all my life I've preferred to be a neighbor to the ghostly." The sign above reads: "The Wealthy Never Tread Here." In the courtyard, birds are caged and fish are kept in bowls, amidst flowers and medicinal herbs. Newly planted banana plants are just the size of a palm; newly added willow trees are already taller than a person. Facing south, there are two rooms, spotlessly clean inside, with a table, a desk, four chairs, two stools, a wooden bed, a bamboo pillow, and a bookshelf. There are also a musical instrument, a sword, and a bamboo stand. On the desk are writing materials: brushes, inkstones, paper, and ink, along with a black silk ruler and a water inkstone, all ready for use. On the wall hangs a painting by the Old Man of Green Vine depicting the myth of Nuwa mending the sky. Nuwa, with a spiral hairstyle and a high forehead, looks up at a furnace, with smoke rising into the sky, her presence vivid and lifelike, clearly an authentic work of art.

    44

    两壁则素粉如银，绝无悬挂。爱极，不问主人谁是，即就榻趺坐。忽一秃发童子自内趋出，视良久，旋诣内，大声呼“有客。”即闻主人在内问讯，命即逐客。所携短僮，殷殷以先生名氏告之，始见主人出，则东坡角巾，王恭鹤氅，羊叔子之缓带，白香山之飞云履，手执麈尾，翩然而来，老叟也。彼此略叙述，语颇投契。问叟名氏，曰：“老夫甄姓，西川人，流寓于此。人以老夫太怪，遂名曰怪叟。”问“富儿绝迹”四字何意，曰：“扬城富儿，近颇好雅，闻老夫居址，小有花草，争来窥瞰。但此辈满身金银气，一入冷境，必多不利，或失足堕溪水，或花刺钩破衣，或遭守门花庞啮破足，或为树杪雀粪污俊庞。

    Explanation

    44

    The walls were painted a pristine white, like silver, with no decorations or hanging items. Entranced, I didn’t care about the owner’s identity and sat cross-legged on the bed. Suddenly, a bald-headed boy emerged from inside, looking at me for a long time before returning inside and shouting loudly, “There’s a guest!” I heard the owner inside inquire and order the guest to be chased away. My young attendant eagerly told the owner my name and title. Finally, the owner appeared, wearing a Su Shi-style cap, a Wang Gong-style robe, a Yang Shuzi-style sash, and Bai Juyi-style flying cloud shoes, holding a fly whisk, and coming in a graceful manner. He was an old man. We exchanged brief words and found each other’s conversation quite congenial. I asked the old man his name, and he replied, “I am from Sichuan and have settled here. People find me too strange and have nicknamed me the Strange Old Man.” I asked about the meaning of the phrase “the rich are nowhere to be seen,” and he said, “The rich in Yangzhou have recently taken to elegance and, hearing about my modest garden, come to look around. But these people, full of the aura of wealth, will face many misfortunes when they enter a cold environment. They might slip and fall into a stream, get pricked by thorns, be bitten by flowers at the gate, or be defecated on by birds high in the trees. That is why I say ‘the rich are nowhere to be seen.’”

    45

    所尤奇者，一日，富儿甫坐定，承尘鼠迹，空隙破瓦堕，正中其额，血淋漓，乃萎顿去。自是相戒，不敢入吾室。遂以为额，志实也。先生清贫则已，若亦富人，恐于先生亦大不利。”先生叹曰：“仆生平亦最恶此辈者。幸福命高，未曾一作富人，得安稳入高斋，领雅教，何幸如之！”须臾，童子献清茗，叟为之鼓琴，风冷冷然，不辨何曲；唯爱其音调激越，渐转和煦，忽铿然顿止。问：“先生能饮乎？”曰：“能。”曰：“盘餐市远无兼味，奈何？”既而自思曰：“釜中狗肉甚烂，然非所以款高贤。”先生性嗜此，闻之垂涎，曰：“仆最喜狗肉，是亦愿狗生八足者。”叟曰：“善。

    45

    The most peculiar thing happened one day when a rich man had just sat down. A tile fell from a gap in the ceiling, landing squarely on his forehead, causing him to bleed profusely before he collapsed and left. From then on, they warned each other and dared not enter my room. Thus, it became a mark, documenting the truth. The master is content with his poverty, but if he were also rich, it would surely be very disadvantageous for him." The master sighed and said, "I have always hated such people the most. Blessed with a high fate, I have never been a rich person, and I have entered the high study room safely, receiving elegant teachings—how fortunate is it!" Soon after, a young boy offered clear tea, and the old man played the zither. The sound was cold and clear, and it was impossible to distinguish what tune it was; he only loved its high-pitched and vigorous tone, which gradually turned gentle and warm, then suddenly stopped sharply. He asked, "Can the master drink?" The master replied, "Yes." The old man said, "The dishes are far away and there are no additional flavors, what to do about it?" After thinking to himself, he said, "The dog meat in the pot is very tender, but it is not suitable to entertain distinguished guests." The master has a strong liking for dog meat, and hearing about it, he drooled, saying, "I love dog meat the most; this is also a wish for a dog to have eight legs." The old man said, "Very well."

    46

    ”即于花下设筵，且啖且饮，狗肉而外，又有山蔬野簌，风味亦佳。叟醉，又抽剑起舞，光缕缕然；未识果否成容，然观其顿挫屈蟠，不减公孙大娘弟子。正白气一团，忽大声跃出圈外，依旧入座，面不改色。先生起敬曰：“翁真高士也！请浮一大白，仆恨相见晚矣！”视日已下山，先生辞退。叟殷殷送过桥曰：“仆与君，同一不合时宜者，如有余暇，可着屐过我。”先生曰：“不速之客，何惜频来！”由是日一过叟，清潭不倦，醉而后返。交月余，渐与谈诗词，皆得妙谛，唯绝口不论书画。先生一日不能忍，告叟曰：“翁亦知某善书画乎？”曰：“不知。”曰：“自信沉迷于此，已三折肱。近今士大夫，颇有嗜痂癖，争致拙作，甚非易事。

    Explanation

    46

    In the midst of the flowers, a banquet was set up, and the old man, after eating and drinking, drew his sword and began to dance. The light from the sword was thread-like, and it was unclear whether it truly became graceful, but observing its pauses and twists, it was no less than the disciples of Gong Sun Da Niang. Suddenly, a mass of white vapor appeared, and the old man shouted loudly and leaped out of the circle, then returned to his seat, his face not changing color. The scholar stood up in respect and said, "Sir, you are truly a high-minded gentleman! Please drink a large cup, I regret meeting you so late!" Seeing that the sun had set, the scholar politely declined. The old man earnestly escorted him across the bridge and said, "If you have spare time, you can wear your clogs and come to visit me." The scholar replied, "An unexpected guest, why not come often!" From then on, he visited the old man every day, never tired of the clear pond, and returned drunk. After several months, they gradually began to talk about poetry and lyrics, gaining profound insights, but never mentioned calligraphy and painting. One day, the scholar could no longer bear it and told the old man, "I am confident that I am deeply engrossed in this, I have already suffered three dislocated arms. Recently, the literati have a taste for something disgusting, strive to obtain my poor works, which is not an easy matter."

    47

    翁素壁既空空，何不以素楮使献所长，亦藉酬东道谊？”曰：“劝君且进一杯。”呼儿磨墨：“楮先生藏之已久，实满眼无一佳士如先生者，故素壁犹虚。顷既相逢，何敢失之交臂。”先生投袂而起，视斋中笔墨纸砚已就，即为挥毫，顷刻十余帧，然后一一书款。叟曰：“小泉乃怪叟字，请赐呼，荣甚。”先生诧曰：“何翁雅人，与贱商某甲同号？”叟曰：“偶相同耳。鲁有两曾参，同名何害？要有清浊之辨耳。”先生信以为实，即书“小泉”二字与之。叟曰：“墨宝非常，从此辉生蓬壁。然不可妄与商人，恐此辈皮相，不能辨珠玉，徒损清名耳。”先生然之。旋又畅饮，归则已二鼓矣。同人问何之，先生盛夸叟。

    Explanation

    47

    The white wall of the old man was already empty, why not use white paper to offer your best work and also reciprocate the host's hospitality?" He said, "Please have another cup of wine." Calling his son to grind ink, he said, "Mr. White Paper has been kept for a long time, but in my eyes, there is not a single good person like you, so the white wall is still empty. Now that we have met, how could I miss this opportunity?" The scholar rose up in excitement, seeing that the ink, brush, paper, and inkstone in the study were ready, he immediately started to write, completing over ten pieces in a short time, then inscribed each one with his name. The old man said, "Little Spring is the strange old man's name, please call me by that name, it would be an honor." The scholar was surprised and said, "What kind of refined gentleman are you, sharing the same name with the lowly merchant Mr. Jia?" The old man replied, "It's just a coincidence. There were two people named Zeng Shen in the state of Lu, having the same name causes no harm. It's all about distinguishing between the clear and the turbid." The scholar believed him and wrote the two characters "Little Spring" for him. The old man said, "Your calligraphy is very valuable, from now on, the wall will shine with brilliance. However, do not give it to merchants at will, for these people have only superficial understanding and cannot distinguish between pearls and jade, it would only damage your good reputation." The scholar agreed. They then drank to their heart's content, and when they returned, it was already the second drumbeat. The colleagues asked where he had gone, and the scholar highly praised the old man.

    48

    众曰：“邗江向无此人。公所见得，无妖魅乎？且彼处丛葬榛莽，向无居人；明当同访，以释其疑。”翌晨，众果偕去，则茅舍全无，唯一湾流水，满地肴核而已。先生大惊，以为遇鬼；旋豁然悟，大叹曰：“商人狡狯，竟能仿萧翼故事，赚我书画耶！”归则使人潜侦，某甲家则已满壁悬挂，墨渖淋漓犹未干也。懊侬氏曰：龙，神物也，风云变幻，天地为冥；人能知其性，且豢之，使俯首就烹割。某甲之设赚局也，布置当行，处处搔着板桥痒处，使彼一齐捧出，毫不吝惜。甲虽市贾，犹是可儿。近则皮相耳食，纯购本，强偷豪窃，几类穿窬。使板桥复生，虽有神龙翔翥之计，又复奈何？余故下一转语曰：人道某甲赚本桥，余道板桥赚某甲。

    Explanation

    48

    The crowd said, "There has never been such a person in Hanjiang before. What you have seen, is there no ghost or spirit? Moreover, that place is overgrown with dense vegetation and contains numerous graves, and there has never been anyone living there; we will visit together tomorrow to dispel their doubts." The next morning, the crowd indeed went together, and they found no thatched cottages, only a bay of water and a ground covered with remnants of food and bones.
