Her mother feared she would commit suicide and went ahead to prevent her. The woman cried and invited the neighbors to sit in the hall, with her mother sitting in a corner of the house. She bowed deeply and said, "My husband has died, and now my mother-in-law has also died. Our clan has no one to continue the lineage, and our family has no close relatives. What use is there for these few rooms and the miscellaneous items? I plan to ask the elders to sell everything on behalf of the family, for my mother-in-law's funeral expenses."

    425

    ”众曰：“善。”遂尽以其资，营斋奠，就窀穸。事毕，女向母拜别，意将雉经，母急抱而止之曰：“儿无姑，尚有母也；儿再死，则我之数根穷饿骨，交付伊谁耶？”由是女始大归，永作依母计。清明寒食，则以一盂麦饭，哭于徐氏墓道。女每号恸，长河之水，则呜咽不流。其族叔某，时劝女再适，女大声屏逐之，不容其刺刺语。旧居老屋，为秋风所破，修葺殊难。时河北中大王庙比丘尼某，亦以名媛披剃，素精戒律，永谢俗尘者，女与谂熟。且先有嫠妇刘妪，亦因前妻子不孝，携己生一幼子、一幼女居焉。

    Explanation

    425

    The crowd said, "Good." Then they used all their resources to prepare offerings and memorial services, proceeding to the burial site. After the funeral was over, the woman bowed to her mother to bid farewell, intending to hang herself. Her mother quickly embraced and stopped her, saying, "My child, you have no grandmother, but you still have a mother; if you die again, who will take care of my poor, starving bones?" Thus, the woman began to return to her natal home, always relying on her mother's plan. During the Qingming and Hanshi festivals, she would offer a bowl of wheat porridge and weep at the tomb path of the Xu family. Whenever she wailed, the water of the long river would sob and not flow. Her cousin's uncle often advised her to remarry, but she shouted loudly to drive him away, refusing to listen to his persistent talk. Her old house had been destroyed by the autumn wind, and it was very difficult to repair. At that time, a nun named a renowned lady at the Zhongdawang Temple in Hebei province had taken monastic vows, always excelling in precepts and permanently renouncing the world. The woman was well acquainted with her. Moreover, there was a widow named Liu, who, because her stepchildren were ungrateful, had taken her own young son and daughter to live there.

    426

    素多苦行，遂奉母行侨寄。尼曰：“一食清斋，颇为不乏，且踵余忏悔可乎？”诸女伴针黹之余，更习讽诵。双柴静掩，灯花寂然。至咸丰十一年，捻匪猖狂，由东鲁席卷而至。时正上元节，大府方衔杯看鳌山灯，听钧天曲，不为防。万骑沓至，官吏始鸟兽散。邑之民遭焚掠掳杀，惨何可言。越三日，贞女辈尚闭户不深悉。会里有被胁者，登垣告之。尼出，果旌帜遍山谷，鼓角作哀音，归急扃其扉，曰：“贼至矣，奈何？”女从容整衣履，跪求母训。母瞠视良久，曰：“若辈犬羊肆淫毒，我且不忍见，况汝乎？行行，吾其从汝志。”刘闻之，亦携女出。

    Explanation

    426

    She often practiced asceticism, so she served her mother by living in temporary lodgings. The nun said, "One meal of vegetarian food is quite sufficient, and can I follow your example in repentance?" The other young women, in their spare time from sewing, also practiced recitation. The double doors were quietly closed, and the lamp flowers were silent. By the eleventh year of the Xianfeng era, the Nian rebels were rampant, rushing from Donglu. It was the Lantern Festival, and the governor was holding a cup, watching the dragon boat lanterns, and listening to celestial music, without taking precautions. Ten thousand cavalry arrived in succession, and the officials began to scatter like birds and beasts. The people of the county suffered burning, looting, abduction, and killing—the suffering is beyond words. After three days, the virtuous women still kept their doors closed and did not deeply understand. A villager who was coerced climbed the wall to inform them. The nun went out, and indeed, flags were everywhere in the valleys, and the drums and horns sounded mournfully. They quickly closed the door, saying, "The bandits have arrived, what shall we do?" The woman calmly dressed herself and knelt to ask for her mother's instructions. The mother stared at her for a long time and said, "These people, like dogs and sheep, commit rampant cruelty. I cannot bear to see it, how much more so you? Go ahead, I will follow your will." Liu heard this and also took his daughter out.

    427

    已同至浅水，见幼子方徘徊岸侧，刘呼之曰：“儿来儿来，儿生必虏，即免，兄嫂亦不可久居，盖相从地下。”儿果奔至。尼见之，抚掌曰：“善哉善哉，是方不愧清静优婆夷也。”于是六人一齐投水死，此胁从者所亲见也。贼，邑之人士闻之，虽未请旌，莫不曰“贞女贞女”云。又数年，一皖南士人，偶至贞女死所，临流瞻眺，忽见一美女子，哭立水面，风鬟雾鬓，仪态万方，手捧赤莲花，冉冉行且歌，歌曰：采莲复采叶，骨似寒冰心似铁；犹记湘垒毕命时，鱼不敢吞，龙不。采莲复采根，生为贞体死贞魂；既与茕独又寇乱，生之杀之天地恩。

    Explanation

    427

    They had already reached the shallow water when they saw the young child wandering by the shore. Liu called out to him, saying, "Come here, come here. You will surely be captured. Even if you escape, your brother and sister-in-law cannot stay long. We will join each other in the afterlife." The child indeed ran over. The nun saw this and clapped her hands, saying, "Excellent, excellent. This one truly lives up to the purity of a female Buddhist layperson." Thus, the six of them all drowned together, which was witnessed by those who were coerced. The bandits were furious, and the townspeople heard about it. Although they did not request an imperial edict, no one did not say, "Chaste woman, chaste woman." Several years later, a scholar from southern Anhui happened to come to the place where the chaste woman died. Standing by the river and gazing, he suddenly saw a beautiful woman crying and standing on the water. Her hair was like wind and mist, and she was graceful and elegant in every way. She held a red lotus in her hand, slowly walked, and sang, singing:

    Picking lotus and lotus leaves, bones like ice, heart like iron.
    Still remembering the time when she met her fate in the Xiang River, fish dare not swallow her, dragon does not.
    Picking lotus and lotus roots, living a chaste body, dying a chaste soul.
    Having been alone and then faced with banditry, life and death are the grace of heaven and earth.

    428

    采莲复采藕，缨络垂珠大如斗；精卫衔冤不敢啼，犹向重泉携母手。采莲复采干，苦海苦海有彼岸；生亦死兮死亦生，太息重呼诸女伴。采莲复采花，彩云一片唤朝霞；金银珠宝有宫阙，帝许贞魂去作家。士人方痴听，忽土人有素识女者，遽呼曰：“此吴贞女耶！”女遗花而杳，亭亭植水面，长可十丈，光烛云霄，移时方乌有。懊侬氏曰：莲者，根劲直而污泥不染，子倒垂而苦心实多，净品也。非九死不回之贞女，谁克当之而无愧也？歌虽近幻，而辞则近悲，作泡影可也，作诔词亦可也。当其月帔风裳，亭亭出水，岂自炫欤？亦以愧夫世之为人臣子，驽恋其位，橐饱其禄，大变当前而贪生怕死者。

    Explanation

    428

    Picking lotus flowers and digging lotus roots, the flowers hang like necklaces with pearls as large as a winnowing basket; Jingwei, bearing her grievance, dares not cry, still holding her mother's hand in the depths of the earth. Picking lotus flowers and digging lotus stems, the bitter sea has its shore; life and death are intertwined, deeply sighing and calling out to all the girls. Picking lotus flowers again, a colorful cloud calls forth the morning glow; gold, silver, jewels, and palaces, the emperor allows the loyal spirit to go home. The scholar is listening in a daze, suddenly, a local person who knew the girl well, quickly called out, "Is this Wu's loyal maiden?" The girl left the flower and disappeared, standing tall on the water, growing up to ten zhang long, its light illuminating the sky, after a while, it vanished. Annoyed Lady said: The lotus has a strong, upright root and is not tainted by the mud, its seeds hang downward with much bitterness, a pure and noble flower. Only a loyal maiden who would not die nine times without returning could face it without shame. The song is nearly illusory, but the words are nearly sorrowful, it can be written as a bubble or as a lament. When she wears a moon robe and a wind dress, standing tall out of the water, is she showing off? Also to shame the world's officials and subjects, who are lazy in their positions, full of their salaries, and greedy for life in the face of great change.

    429

    古泗州城
    　　
    　　吾乡泗州城，沦为洪泽湖久矣。土人云为大禹命庚辰所系水怪巫支祈逸出为害，此无稽也。州城之沉乃明末事，其时画士恽南田正寓僧伽禅寺。门前一水环绕，出入须楫。时已四十五日雨，淮流七十二道山溪之水，全归于此。童谣早有“石龟滴血泪，要命上东山”之语，恽甚忧之。夜静，偶闻神鬼满堂私议，曰：“时已至矣，乞施行。”神曰：“尚有一僧一道未归，一主一仆未出，姑须臾。”恽披衣起，殿黑无人，知水厄至，急呼仆起，携随身文具，仓皇拔关出走。过渡，见庙僧携杖打包归，曰：“先生何往？”曰：“吾有急，须登第一山耳。”所谓第一山者，盱山也。主仆踯躅，甫逾岭，天遽明，回头一眺，则白茫茫一片水国，成巨浸矣。

    Explanation

    429

    The Ancient City of Sizhou

    My hometown, the ancient city of Sizhou, has been submerged in Hongze Lake for a long time. The locals say it was submerged due to a water demon named Wuzhiqi, who was released by Emperor Yu the Great's command, causing harm. This is baseless. The city's submersion was an event during the late Ming dynasty. At that time, the painter Yun Nantian was residing in the Sengga Zen Temple. There was a water body surrounding the temple entrance, and entry and exit required a boat. For forty-five days, the waters of the Huai River, flowing from seventy-two mountain streams, all converged here. There was an early nursery rhyme that said, "The stone turtle drips blood tears, taking one's life to the east mountain." Yun Nantian was very worried about this. During the quiet night, one occasionally heard gods and ghosts privately discussing, saying, "The time has come, begging for action." The god said, "There is still a monk and a Taoist priest who have not returned, and a master and his servant have not left. Wait a moment." Yun Nantian got up in his clothes, finding the hall dark and empty, knowing that a water disaster was imminent. He urgently called the servant to wake up, took along personal writing tools, and hurriedly broke through the gate and fled. Crossing the water, he saw the temple monk returning with a staff and packed belongings. The master and servant hesitated, just crossing the mountain ridge, the sky suddenly became bright. Looking back in a glance, they saw a vast expanse of white water, becoming a large lake.

    430

    由明季至昭代，沉沦已数百年，从未出现，惟阴雨中，时有雉堞排水上，台榭人物，如蜃气馔成，盖幻象也。咸丰六年大旱，湖涸而城基出，好事者舣舟步入，则官庙宇基址，尚觉依稀；路石女墙，犹存其半。又有半塔耸高处，啮缺非常，闻即僧伽惮寺塔，南将军乞师贺兰时所射者。人取其砖琢砚，甚古朴有致，惜砖质不能细腻耳。有陕客乘舟经此，天暮，见有大滩，即系而止宿，实不知其为古泗州也。夜静月如昼，客舍舟登岸，独行破寂。忽见高处有城垣，门半掩，悄睨之，中有灯火，人物往来，贸易若夜市。客本贾人，见之心喜，掩入，随步所之，见各家门户悬有灯彩，生涯，尤为纷攘。然已掩其旁，人若不之见；试攫其物，人亦如不见。大骇。

    Explanation

    430

    From the late Ming to the Qing era, this place had been submerged and forgotten for several hundred years, never appearing until it occasionally surfaced during rainy weather. Remnants of city walls could be seen on the water, along with pavilions and people, as if formed by mirage—these were mere illusions. In the sixth year of the Xianfeng era, a great drought occurred, causing the lake to dry up and revealing the foundations of the city. Curious individuals moored their boats and stepped ashore, finding the foundations of official temples still vaguely visible; stone paving and parapets remained half intact. There was also a half-tower standing tall in a high place, with unusual damage. It was said to be the temple tower of Sizhou, shot by a general seeking help from Helan. People took the bricks to carve into writing stones, which were very ancient and simple but tasteful, though unfortunately, the quality of the bricks could not be fine-grained. A guest from Shaanxi was traveling by boat through the area when it got dark, and he saw a large sandbank. He immediately tied up his boat and stopped to stay overnight, not knowing that it was the ancient city of Sizhou. The night was quiet, and the moon was as bright as day. The guest left the boat and went ashore, walking alone and breaking the silence. Suddenly, he saw a city wall in a high place, with the door half-open. He peered at it quietly and saw lights inside, with people and things moving around, trading as if it were a night market. Being a merchant himself, he felt happy upon seeing this. He stepped in and followed the path, seeing each family's door with lanterns hanging. The life was very busy and bustling. However, he was already hidden on the side, and people as if they did not see him; when he tried to take something, people also as if they did not see him. He was greatly frightened.

    431

    视茧布贵重者，攫而怀之，急向西。又见一家门微启，孤檠若灭。潜入视之，则一家男妇，皆支颐卧，唤不应，视妇貌酷似其妇；男子貌又酷似己。以为貌偶同，不之异。睹架椟藏银处，并无封志，启之则朱提满焉。心艳之，急取二百金置怀袖间，余仍闭诸椟，并代掩双扉出。见人家灯火渐阑，市渐散。恐闭城，不敢逗留，趋出登舟，倚枕骇异，旋入黑甜。比醒，则舟子已先起，晨抵盱眙矣。视所攘茧布，已成泥淤，杂芹藻成饼。视所怀朱提，则翘边细纹，的真宝物。唯其色暗淡，水花苔几满。私以昨宵宿处，询舟子，约略言之，云其中绝无人烟，心更惊怖，不知所云。

    Explanation

    431

    Seeing the valuable silk, he grabbed it and hid it, then hurried westward. He then saw a slightly ajar door with a dimly lit lamp. He crept inside to look and found a man and woman both leaning on their cheeks, fast asleep. He called out to them, but they did not respond. The woman's appearance was very similar to his wife's, and the man's appearance was very similar to his own. Thinking that the resemblance was coincidental, he did not suspect anything. He saw a chest where silver was stored, which was not sealed. When he opened it, it was full of Zhu Ti gold. Coveting it, he quickly took two hundred gold pieces and placed them in his sleeves, leaving the rest still locked in the chests, and closed the double doors before leaving. He saw that the lights in people's homes were gradually fading and the market was dispersing. Afraid that the city gates would close, he dared not linger, hurried out and boarded the boat, leaning on the pillow in shock, and soon fell into a deep sleep. When he woke up, the boatman had already gotten up, and they arrived in Xuyi in the morning. Looking at the stolen silk, it had already turned to mud and silt, mixed with water plants and formed into a cake. Looking at the gold he had stolen, it had fine edges and patterns, indeed a true treasure. However, its color was dull, almost covered with water stains and moss. Privately asking the boatman about where he had stayed the previous night, the boatman gave a vague account, saying there was no human habitation there. His heart was even more frightened, and he did not know what to say.

    432

    秋后回陕，偶与其妻言之，妻曰：“大奇，妾于某月日夜梦至一城，与君一处卧，甫交睫，君忽起，开椟怀银出，妾惊异，尾之行，君忽不见，妾心汗涔涔不已。”盖其妻入梦之夜，即此客人入城之时，默忆旧游，丝毫不爽。又盱眙向无大圣庙，大圣者，即僧伽也。粤匪窜后，当道者谕揖修考院。工竣，尚余三千金，公议建大圣庙。上梁日，适朱学使考毕，亲诣拈香，鼓吹喧阗，冠盖云集。忽一渔人捧一古铜鼎送庙来供养，云昨宵见湖心有光，一网举出者。洗涤审视，口内有篆文，盖唐时僧伽庙中物也。学使大喜，亲为其文纪其事。懊侬氏曰：俗云：“钱财通性命。”此公隔世，犹重到故居，亲携阿堵而出。观此，益信其言之不诬也。而蜃余沙碛，市井何来？眼底烟云，门庭依旧，又何故与？佛云：“如梦幻泡形，如露亦如电”。明白了当而言之，惜世人不悟耳！

    Explanation

    432

    After the autumn, he returned to Shaanxi and chanced to speak with his wife. She said, "It is greatly strange. On a certain night of a certain month, I dreamed of arriving at a city and lying with you in one place. Just as soon as I closed my eyes, you suddenly got up, opened a box, and took out silver. I was shocked and followed you. You suddenly disappeared, and my heart was drenched in sweat, unable to stop." It happened that the night when his wife entered her dream was the same time when this guest entered the city. She silently recalled past travels, and not a single detail was wrong. Also, Xuyi previously had no Great Saint Temple. The Great Saint is none other than the Bodhisattva Samantabhadra. After the Taiping rebels fled, the local officials ordered the construction of an examination hall. When the work was completed, there were still three thousand taels of silver left. They publicly agreed to build a Great Saint Temple. On the day when the ridgepole was erected, just as the provincial examiner finished the examination, he personally came to burn incense. There was loud music and noise, and a gathering of officials and dignitaries. Suddenly a fisherman carried an ancient bronze tripod to the temple for veneration. He said he saw light in the middle of the lake last night and caught it with a single net. After washing and examining it, there were inscriptions inside the mouth. It was an object from the Temple of Samantabhadra in the Tang Dynasty. The provincial examiner was very pleased and personally wrote an inscription to record the event. The author, Aonong, said: "As the saying goes, 'Money can influence one's fate.'" This person, after a lifetime, still returned to his former home and personally took out the money. Seeing this, one can further believe that the saying is not false. But where did the market come from in the sandy desert? The view before one's eyes is like smoke and clouds, but the courtyard remains the same. And why is this so? The Buddha said, "Like a dream, like a mirage, like a bubble, like dew, like lightning." Clearly and plainly stated, it is a pity that the world does not understand.

    433

    独角兽
    　　
    　　兴化有丐者，乞食廛市间，观者围之如堵墙。余曾亦趋视，盖其人顶生一角，矗正中，根束短发如毛，角首微锐而梢，朽如蠹啮。人争问讯，泫然曰：“余非生而戴角者。幼不肖，动辄忤双亲，攫资宿妓，家贫，双亲啖糠而已，犹携酒肴就妓家食，尤喜煎炒脆炸，浓汁厚味。亲死，以芦包葬东郭，甫归家，正遭回禄，屋宇什物，一时煨烬。贫不能耐，则于亲族友党中唆是非，挑械斗，兴词讼，己则假为调停，攫鹬蚌利。是年，年三十有二，忽病，自分必死。数日，颅肉痒甚，抓挠肤裂，紫血涔涔。突一角出，如笋掀泥。自顾镜中，已似神羊。

    Explanation

    433

    A Unicorn

    In Xinghua, there was a beggar who begged for food in the market, and the onlookers gathered around him like a wall. I once went to see him, and it turned out that the man had a horn growing on top of his head, standing upright in the center. The base of the horn was bound by short hair like bristles, and the tip was slightly sharp while the end was worn and decayed, as if gnawed by insects. People gathered to ask about him, and he wept and said, "I was not born with a horn. In my youth, I was unworthy and often defied my parents, seizing money to stay with prostitutes. My family was poor, and my parents ate only bran, yet I still brought wine and food to the brothel. I particularly enjoyed fried and rich foods. After my parents died, they were wrapped in reeds and buried in the eastern suburb. Just as I returned home, a fire broke out, and my house and belongings were instantly burned to ashes. Unable to bear the poverty, I stirred up trouble among relatives and friends, provoked fights, and initiated lawsuits. I pretended to mediate, seizing the advantage of the disputes. That year, at the age of thirty-two, I suddenly fell ill and thought I would surely die. After several days, my scalp itched intensely, and I scratched it until my skin split, with purple blood flowing. Suddenly, a horn emerged, like a bamboo shoot breaking through the mud. When I looked at myself in the mirror, I already resembled a divine sheep.

    434

    而奇痛不可忍，呼号床第间。十余日，忽梦一神人告之曰：‘尔思病从何来？角从何生？曷言其故，痛自己。’寐而自省，试以己之罪恶，于人前姑言之，痛果已。顷年已六十，如故，一日不言，一日便痛。”言已，唏嘘泪下如雨。人怜之，多施一文钱，由是得无馁。乡中小儿，偶摩娑其角而动摇之，便哀呼诵佛号，自云：“兴化东乡人，无名氏，惟号独角兽。”余戏为摹小像，赞云：“彼何人斯，乞于东郭；身未披毛，首先戴角；春笋怒芽，上指寥廓。其身犹人，其心则兽。兽耶人耶，峨峨穿透。地狱人间，黄泉白昼。非獬豸冠，亦非角端。铙鼎不铸，山经不刊。好角逐者，蓦见心寒。”

    Explanation

    434

    The strange pain was unbearable, and he shouted and called out in the bedroom. For more than ten days, he suddenly dreamed of a divine person who told him, "Think about where your illness comes from? Where does your horn grow? How can you explain this, the pain is self-inflicted." Waking up and reflecting on himself, he tried to confess his own sins in front of others, and indeed the pain subsided. Recently turned sixty, he remained as before, if he didn't speak for a day, he would suffer pain for a day. After speaking, he sighed and cried like rain. People pitied him and often gave him a copper coin, thus he did not go hungry. Local children occasionally touched and moved his horn, and he would then cry out and recite Buddhist mantras, saying, "I am from the eastern village of Xinghua, nameless, only known as the Unicorn." I jokingly drew a small portrait and wrote an inscription: "Who is this person, begging in the eastern suburbs; his body is still human, but his heart is that of a beast. Is he an animal or a human? His presence pierces through. Between hell and earth, between the netherworld and daylight. Neither a magistrate's hat nor a unicorn's horn. Not cast in a bell or tripod, not recorded in the Shan Jing (Classic of Mountains and Seas). Those who like to compete suddenly feel cold."

    435

    夜雨秋灯录　三集
    　　
    　　清　宣鼎
    　　
    　　卷一
    　　
    　　先觉僧
    　　
    　　朔平陆太守，浙人也。其恭人好佛，得一玉大士像，至诚供奉，持诵《大悲咒》甚虔。中年举一子，自幼茹素，三四岁，随母礼拜，诵咒则喜。使之入塾，则瞑目枯坐，父师以其幼也，未之责备。八岁时，太守强其食肉，大呕尽吐，即心厌尘凡矣。窃母白金八十，望五台而逸。相距不远，竟日而至。向僧礼拜，求为剃度；僧不知来历，何敢收之。乃遍拜坐禅者。内有游方和尚，同乡人也，悯其幼小无依，愿为带回故里。

    Explanation

    435

    The Night Rain and Autumn Lantern Records, Volume Three
    　　
    　　By Xuan Ding of the Qing Dynasty
    　　
    　　Volume One
    　　
    　　The Awakened Monk
    　　
    　　The governor of Shuoping, a native of Zhejiang, had a wife who was devout in Buddhism. She obtained a jade statue of a Bodhisattva and worshipped it with utmost sincerity, reciting the Great Compassion Mantra with great devotion. In her middle age, she gave birth to a son who abstained from meat from a young age. At the age of three or four, he followed his mother in worship and was happy to recite the mantra. When he was sent to school, he would sit motionless with closed eyes, and his father and teachers, considering his young age, did not reprimand him. At the age of eight, the governor forced him to eat meat, and he vomited it all up, immediately developing a strong aversion to the mundane world. He stole eighty taels of silver from his mother and fled to Wutai Mountain. It was not far away, and he arrived there at the end of the day. He bowed to the monks and asked to be ordained; the monks, unsure of his background, dared not accept him. He then bowed to and sat in meditation with other monks. Among them was a wandering monk who was from the same hometown, who pitied the boy's young age and lack of support and wished to take him back to their hometown.

    436

    苦求为僧，相携至天台；求善知识，为之摹顶授记，赐予法名，曰先觉。师问之曰：“浮屠之学，不出教禅二端。所谓教者，唯识宗旨，即三藏佛经九千五百卷，以持诵而论证果者也。所谓禅者，东来初祖达摩大师，宏教外别传之旨，不立文字，而见性成佛。其后派虽分五宗，不出静定而已。汝愿何学？”先觉曰：“弟子不识字，何必受文字之障，愿归禅门。”乃授以秘密法旨，守之无失，唯精唯勤。至十八岁，能入定数十日，历受三道戒，得衣钵度牒，别师外出云游。

    Explanation

    436

    The translation is as follows:

    He sought desperately to become a monk, and they accompanied him to the TianTai region; he sought a good teacher, who bestowed a blessing by tracing his head, granted him a Dharma name, called Xianjue. The master asked him, "Buddhist learning does not exceed the two aspects of teaching and meditation. What is called teaching is the doctrine of consciousness-only, which is the three baskets of Buddhist scriptures totaling 9,500 volumes, those who hold and recite the scriptures to attain enlightenment. What is called meditation is the first patriarch of Buddhism who came from the East, Master Bodhidharma, who widely propagated the teachings outside the scriptures, without relying on written words, and realizing one's true nature to become a Buddha. Although the schools later divided into five, they do not exceed stillness and tranquility. What do you wish to study?" Xianjue replied, "I cannot read, so why should I be hindered by written words? I wish to return to the Chan school." The master then bestowed upon him secret teachings, advising him to guard them without loss, to be diligent and focused. By the age of eighteen, he could enter meditation for dozens of days, having received the three precepts, and obtained the robe, bowl, and ordination certificate. He left his master and traveled far and wide as a wandering monk.

    437

    至武林之灵隐寺，遇异僧溷迹于火工者，遇人不言不笑。见先觉，邀之入室。自言原大唐时人，历今千余岁矣，立愿食亿万僧，而后圆正果，是以在香积厨作食。且曰：“我与汝有前缘，故告汝，此非栖息之地，以北去为善。”先觉求其指示，则曰：“我除执爨外，无法可传。”乃别去。先觉遵其教，欲去，忽遇旧仆再拜曰：“公子何往？先尊与太夫人，自失公子后，悲痛甚，四处招寻。逾五六载，相继而逝。大公子扶柩旋里。

    Explanation

    437

    To the Lingyin Temple in West Lake, Xianjue encountered a strange monk who was working as a fireworker in the temple kitchen. The monk neither spoke nor smiled to people. When Xianjue met him, the monk invited him into his room. The monk introduced himself, saying he was originally from the Tang Dynasty and had lived for over a thousand years. He had made a vow to eat billions of monks and then achieve enlightenment, which is why he worked in the Fragrant Heap kitchen. He added, "I have a karmic connection with you, so I am telling you: this is not a place to settle, and it is better to go north." Xianjue asked for his guidance, and the monk replied, "Besides cooking, I have no teachings to pass on." Then he left. Xianjue followed the monk's advice and prepared to depart. Suddenly, he encountered an old servant who bowed twice and said, "Master, where are you going? After losing you, my late father and mother were very sorrowful and searched everywhere. After five or six years, they passed away one after another. The eldest son carried the coffin back home."

    438

    缘先太尊在日，曾为公子聘冯御史女，今已及笄，催完姻者屡矣。大公子遣奴辈多人相觅，小人幸遇，必请公子同归。”先觉知父母已过，大恸几绝。随家人回见乃兄，劝之易服毕姻。谓新妇曰：“我与汝了此一段姻缘，数乃前定。但我已证罗汉果，不通人道，留此无益。我从此行矣，去留由汝。”新妇亦不挽留，曰：“我知之已久，亦将皈依佛法。”先觉授之以禅，乃携母遗之女大仕像，不别而行。北走燕山之西域寺，爱其岩洞幽寂，扶藤而下，面溪背壁，趺坐于中。日有寺内人来给食。坐逾两载，功益精进。

    438

    When my late father was alive, he once arranged for the young master to marry the daughter of a censor. She is now of marriageable age, and there have been many attempts to complete the marriage. The eldest son sent many servants to search for her, and I was fortunate to meet them. I will certainly ask the young master to return home together. The young master, upon learning that his parents had passed away, was overwhelmed with grief and nearly fainted. He returned with his family to see his elder brother, who urged him to change into wedding attire and complete the marriage. He said to his new wife, "I and you have completed this marriage, the fate was predetermined. But I have attained the state of Arhat, I no longer engage in human relations, it is of no use for me to stay here. I will leave now, whether to stay or go is up to you." The new wife did not try to persuade him to stay, saying, "I have known this for a long time, I will also seek refuge in the Buddhist path." The young master taught her the Dharma, then carried the image of his deceased mother's daughter-in-law, and left without bidding farewell. He headed north to the Western Paradise Temple on Yanshan Mountain, loved its secluded and quiet rock cave, and descended by holding onto vines, facing the stream and with the wall behind, sat cross-legged in the center. Daily, people from the temple brought food, and he sat for more than two years, progressing increasingly.

    439

    忽闻车轰雷吼之声，山谷振动，见大蟒探首欲相食。先觉宁神静摄，听之而已。顷刻无蟒。次日复然。自忖此地缘满，故妖魔起，遂入都。经历各寺，未遇高人，终日静定而已，眉目间光华灿烂，迥别凡流。诸王与公卿，见而异之，共保举入万寿寺方丈。坐年余，复布施万余金，曰：“此势利地，非修真所也，不可堕落。”乃封其金于库而夜遁。爰朝五台，出嘉峪关，历西域，寻访佛迹，到人所罕到之处，无所遇而还。游终南山，遍拜诸寺。

    Explanation

    439

    Suddenly, he heard the thunderous roar of a vehicle, causing the mountains and valleys to tremble. He saw a large python extending its head, intent on devouring him. The wise person calmed and concentrated, listening to it all. In an instant, the python was gone. The next day, it happened again. He thought this place was full of karma, hence demons arose, and he then entered the capital. He visited various temples but did not meet any great person, spending the whole day in quiet meditation. The brilliance of light shone in his eyes and brows, differing greatly from ordinary people. Kings and high-ranking officials saw him and were amazed, jointly recommending him to be the abbot of the Wanshou Temple. He served for more than a year, again donating tens of thousands of gold, saying, "This is a place of power and profit, not a place for true cultivation, and one cannot fall into corruption." He sealed the gold in the treasury and fled at night. Then he visited Mount Wutai, left the Jiayu Pass, traveled through the Western Regions, sought the traces of the Buddha, reaching places rarely visited by people, but found nothing and returned. He visited Mount Zhongnan, thoroughly visiting all the temples.

    440

    佥云在山之阳，其山阴，相隔黄流，望之，树木阴森，无敢入者。即有好事之人，去亦不返，相传为栖真之所。先觉欣然愿往。或阻之，不听，携带干粮，渡河而入。见古木万株，参天覆地，高皆数寻，枝叶相接，其下朦朦胧胧，微可辨路而已。先觉由之，日则攀葛结藤，夜则调息入定。约行七八日，始出树林，则现奇峰叠障，千态万状，或以高，或崭而削，或以幽，或崎岖而险，径路盘曲，似有人行者。复数日，出山坳，则势更，莫穷莫尽，然皆秀色可餐，无虎狼恶兽患。

    Explanation

    440

    It is said that on the sunny side of the mountain, its shady side is separated by the Yellow River. From afar, the trees appear dense and dark, and no one dares to enter. Even those who are curious venture in but do not return, and it is said to be a place where true nature or spiritual beings reside. The enlightened one eagerly wishes to go. Some try to stop him, but he does not listen. Carrying dry food, he crosses the river and enters. He sees countless ancient trees, towering and widespread, with heights of several zhang, their branches and leaves interconnecting, and the ground below dim and indistinct, barely allowing for the discernment of a path. The enlightened one proceeds, climbing vines and tying them together during the day, and regulating his breath and entering a meditative state at night. After about seven or eight days, he finally emerges from the forest, revealing unusual and layered mountains, with thousands of forms and appearances, some high, some sharp and steep, some secluded, and some rugged and dangerous. The paths wind and twist, as if there are people walking. After several more days, he emerges from a valley, and the scenery changes, endless and boundless, yet all beautiful enough to eat, with no danger from tigers, wolves, or other wild beasts.

    441

    遥望山腰，时见石龛，隐隐有人趺坐。第四面峭壁，无路可通，不知从何处登也。先觉呼之不应，膜拜之，亦不答。又无水可饮，虽闻潺潺之声，而溪皆深邃，不得下，乃有退心。迎面一古衣冠道人来，先觉欢欣相接，牵其衣裾而拜，尊之曰“大仙。”其人笑曰：“予凡夫耳，焉得仙？仙人肯使汝见耶？”相与就石凳共坐，叩所从来。其人曰：“予世居越国，徐姓，秦皇时为役夫，是楚汉纷争，投楚而卒，乌江之败，逃入此山，不复出矣。

    Explanation

    441

    Looking into the mountain, one occasionally sees stone niches, and faintly perceives someone sitting cross-legged. The fourth side is a sheer cliff, with no path to pass through, and one does not know where to climb from. The awakened one calls out but gets no response, kneels and prostrates himself, but he also does not respond. There is also no water to drink, although hearing the sound of the stream, but the streams are all deep and secluded, cannot descend, thus having the thought of retreating. A man of antiquity with ancient attire comes face to face, the awakened one rejoices to meet, pulling his garment and bowing, respecting him and calling him "Great Immortal." The man smiles and says, "I am just an ordinary man, how can I be an immortal? Would the Immortal allow you to see him?" Together they sit on a stone bench, inquiring about where he comes from. The man says, "I have lived in Yue State, last name Xu, during the Qin Emperor's time, I was a laborer, it was during the Chu-Han contention, joined Chu and died, the defeat at the Black River, fled into this mountain, never came out again."

    442

    ”先觉告以渴甚，乃身解佩匙，即于石山挖之，得水与饮。清凉渗膏，连给二匙，收藏欲去。先觉曰：“弟子尚未足也。”其人曰：“不但汝渴已已，即不食亦无碍矣，何必多饮。”先觉犹持裾不释，哀求超度。其人曰：“道不同，不相为谋。我非汝师，汝必欲度，不见前山又有人来耶？”先觉回首间，一阵清风，绝裾而去。手中仅留青蓝布一握，其人不知何往。乃以布裹所携之玉大士像。自饮石髓后，觉身体益轻健，不食不饥，日行四五百里。遂出终南，游山左，不复反。或曰“在青莱之间，山深不知处耳。”

    Explanation

    442

    The Enlightened One told him that he was very thirsty. He immediately removed his jade spoon and dug into the stone mountain, finding water and drinking it. The water was cool and refreshing, seeping like oil, and he gave him two ladles full. He collected it and wanted to leave. The Enlightened One said, "The disciple is not yet satisfied." The person replied, "Not only are you thirsty, but even if you don’t eat, it won’t matter. Why drink so much?" The Enlightened One still held onto his robe, pleading for salvation. The person said, "Different paths, different plans. I am not your teacher. If you must be saved, don’t you see another person coming from the front mountain?" The Enlightened One turned around, and a gust of clear wind blew, tearing the hem of his robe as he left. He left only a handful of blue-green cloth in his hand, and the person did not know where he went. He then wrapped the jade statue of the Great Master he was carrying with the cloth. After drinking the stone marrow, he felt his body becoming lighter and healthier, not feeling hungry even without eating, and he could travel four or five hundred miles in a day. He then left Mount Zhongnan, wandered to the left of the mountains, and never returned. Some say that he is somewhere between Qing and Lai, deep in the mountains, and no one knows where exactly.

    443

    华疯子
    　　
    　　江右华三祝，幼名多官，其父母晚年得之，溺爱过甚，听其所为。读书不就，亦不事别业，终日游荡而已。听鸣钲开道者过，随之入衙，观其升堂理事，詈人挞人，皆不敢违拗，心甚艳之。于是归家演习，亲友俱以疯子目之。逢人则问官何来，有诳之者曰：“官易事耳，但须北京去见皇帝，求之即得。”乃窃其父金数两，往北逸去。至浦口，一无所有矣。遇官舫之赴楚者，觅纤手，昧然应募。日行百里，唯问北京何时可到，众皆知其疯，皆玩戏之。惟官舫中一叟，屡目顾之，周其衣食。

    Explanation

    443

    Hua the Madman

    From Jiangxi, there was a man named Hua Sanzhu, whose childhood name was Duo Guan. His parents had him late in life and doted on him excessively, allowing him to do as he pleased. He did not succeed in his studies and did not engage in any other profession, spending his days wandering aimlessly. He would follow the sound of the bell being rung to clear the way for officials, entering the government office to watch them ascend the hall to handle affairs, scold and beat others, with no one daring to disobey. He greatly admired these actions. Thus, he returned home to practice, and his relatives and friends all regarded him as a madman. Whenever he met someone, he would ask where the official came from. Someone deceived him, saying, "Being an official is easy; one must go to Beijing to see the emperor and request it." So, he stole several taels of gold from his father and fled north. Upon arriving at Pu Kou, he had nothing left. He encountered an official boat heading to Chu, volunteered to pull the boat, and blindly accepted the offer. Traveling a hundred li each day, he only asked when he could reach Beijing. Everyone knew he was crazy and treated him as a joke. Only an elderly man on the official boat often looked at him and took care of his clothing and food.

    444

    一日，遇飓风，停泊河畔。华在小庙檐下坐卧，忽叟来邀之入庙，密语之曰：“舫内之官，是吾子张某，其貌与汝无异，即至亲亦难辨认。以吏员铨得楚县尉，至楚赴任，不意昨得暴疾卒矣。我与眷属，贫不能回家。依我计议，可将汝衣，与我子互易，汝承我子职，官中所得，割半均分，则两全其事矣。”华如其所愿，疯疾顿愈，是夜潜相易换。明日，舟子唤疯子，则见其倒毙于路矣，叟为买棺厝之，遂赴任。分衙署为两宅，与眷属各居。嫂事张妻，叟为经理出入。荏苒三年，各分得千金。

    Explanation

    444

    One day, encountering a typhoon, Hua anchored by the riverbank. Sitting and lying under the eaves of a small temple, he was suddenly invited into the temple by an old man who whispered to him, "The official in the boat is my son Zhang Mou. His appearance is no different from yours, even close relatives would find it hard to distinguish. He was promoted to the position of county magistrate in Chu through the examination system. He went to take up his post in Chu but unexpectedly died of a sudden illness yesterday. I and my family are poor and cannot return home. Follow my plan, you can exchange clothes with my son. You will take over my son's position, and we will split the salary equally, thus both of us will benefit." Hua agreed to the plan, and his madness suddenly healed. That night, they secretly exchanged clothes. The next day, when the boatman called for the madman, he found him lying dead on the road. The old man bought a coffin and buried him, and then took up his post. They divided the government office into two residences, and the family members lived separately. The wife of Zhang's brother-in-law served Zhang's wife, and the old man managed the household affairs. After three years, each of them received a thousand gold pieces.

    445

    忽闻新太守莅任，探知为龚姓，系华氏姑母之子，以科甲由部曹出守是邦者。难与相见，然在其属下，不敢不谒。是日，以张姓职名入见。太守睹其状貌，核其履历，心甚疑之。入见太夫人，言其故。太夫人泣曰：“尔舅父仅有一子，不知何往，吾亦欲观其貌。纵非真者，藉以解渴想耳。”太守唯唯，立命传见。华俯首攒眉，置身无地，惟有叩首而已。太夫人命仆扶之起，详加体察，曰：“是吾侄也。奈何勿认？”华益惶恐，曰：“野鸟何敢冒凤。貌相似者，自古有之；孔子阳虎，圣狂不类耳。”即告退。

    Explanation

    445

    Suddenly, it was heard that the new magistrate had arrived in office. It was learned that he was surnamed Gong and was the son of my aunt from the Hu family, who had been promoted from a central government position to govern this region through the imperial examination system. It was difficult to meet him, but since he was under my command, I could not avoid paying respects. On that day, I went to see the magistrate's mother and explained the reason. The magistrate's mother wept and said, "Your uncle has only one son, and I do not know where he is. I also want to see his appearance, even if it is not the real one, just to relieve my longing." The magistrate nodded in agreement and immediately ordered to bring the person in. The person lowered his head and furrowed his brows, feeling at a loss, and could only bow his head. The magistrate's mother had a servant help him up and carefully examined him, saying, "This is my nephew. Why not recognize him?" The person became increasingly fearful and said, "A wild bird dares not pretend to be a phoenix. It has always been the case that appearances can be similar; Confucius and Yang Hu are so different that they cannot be compared." Then he left.

    446

    太夫人未能挽留，目送之，将出门，忽呼其幼名曰：“多官儿，安在耶？”华闻之，不觉回顾。太夫人即使仆婢，群拥之入，作色视之，曰：“汝是吾侄华三祝也，何得背父母，瞒亲戚，好官自为耶？”华窘极，叩首，曰：“非也。”太夫人曰：“吾自呼多官儿，何劳汝回首？再欲强辨，吾命汝表兄以官法治汝矣。”华至是无奈，直陈始末。太守骇曰：“此事性命相关，弟奈何为之？其速回乃任，与张叟交割，我以公事参革弟，即嘱张叟代报病故，潜回家乡，我为弟以真姓名别纳一官可也。”华从之，得以父子团聚，保其首领以殁。

    Explanation

    446

    The mother-in-law was unable to retain him and watched as he departed. As he was about to leave, she suddenly called out to him by his childhood name: "Where are you, my dear?" Hearing this, Hu turned back unconsciously. The mother-in-law then had the servants and maids gather to bring him inside and looked at him sternly, saying, "You are my nephew Hu Sanzhu, how could you betray your parents, deceive your relatives, and seek office for yourself?" Hu was completely at a loss and bowed deeply, saying, "No, it's not true." The mother-in-law said, "I called you 'my dear,' why did you turn back? If you insist on arguing further, I will have your cousin deal with you according to official law." Hu, having no choice, told the whole story straightforwardly. The magistrate was shocked and said, "This matter involves life and death, how could you do this? Return quickly to your post and hand over to Mr. Zhang. I will report you for official misconduct and instruct Mr. Zhang to report your death. You can return secretly to your hometown, and I will arrange for you to hold an official position under your real name." Hu followed his instructions and was able to reunite with his father, preserving his head until death.

    447

    补骗子十二则
    　　
    　　京师某王，因公受罚，缘是致贫，众所知也。时值创建大寺，布施已成，唯乏殿材，匠人各处购求。乃有随官服食者，登大匠之门，告曰：“我为某王府四品护卫。今王当窘急之际，欲货其殿廷旧料，易以轻巧之木，冀得余资以济急需。”匠知王为开国勋裔，其府第皆梓楠为之，欣然愿售，约日往观。其人以亲王名柬至王府，告门官曰：“我为某王护卫，今王欲新殿廷，慕府内规模宏大，谕我带同匠人观之，以便如式构造。”王许之，乃邀匠人入。

    Explanation

    447

    Translation:

    Twelve Rules for Dealing with Swindlers

    In the capital, there was a prince who, due to a public punishment, became impoverished, a fact known to all. At this time, he was building a great temple, and the alms-giving had been completed, but only lacked materials for the main hall, which the craftsmen were purchasing from various places. Then, a person who followed officials and their lifestyle came to the door of the chief craftsman and said, "I am a fourth-rank guard of a certain princely household. Now that the prince is in a dire situation, he wants to sell the old materials from the main hall and replace them with lighter wood, hoping to obtain extra funds to meet urgent needs." The craftsmen knew that the prince was a descendant of the founding nobility, and his mansion was made of zelkova and camphor wood, so they gladly agreed to buy the materials and agreed on a date to inspect them. The person went to the princely household with a letter of introduction from the prince and told the gatekeeper, "I am a guard of a certain prince. Now the prince wants to renovate the main hall, admiring the grand scale of the mansion, and instructed me to bring the craftsmen to see it, so they can construct according to the model." The prince agreed, and invited the craftsmen to enter.

    448

    指点其梁楹，筹度其丈尺，详细阅毕，偕至匠寓评价。其人曰：“先王成此殿，费十万金，汝愿以若干售之，不妨明言。”匠人曰：“材料已旧，大而无当，将必改为小用，不过万金而已。”其人故作不愿。往来数次，曰：“王今无奈，姑以售汝，当在某庄立券，汝先往俟之。”是日，朱轮华毂，王果至庄。其人为前驱，带匠人入庄。王南面坐，匠跽请书券。王颔之，命从官书就，亲笔签押，先索定契银三千两，余俟折换之日准算。其人偕匠入城交银，并索费，亦先给三百，约日而散。至日，大匠带人往拆殿廷，门官拒之。

    Explanation

    448

    The craftsman pointed out the beams and pillars, measured the dimensions, and after a detailed inspection, they went together to the craftsman's residence for evaluation. The person said, "The former king built this hall, costing ten thousand gold. How much do you wish to sell it for? Feel free to state it openly." The craftsman replied, "The materials are old, large but impractical, and will certainly be repurposed for smaller uses, so it is worth no more than ten thousand gold." The person feigned reluctance. After several visits, he said, "The king is now helpless, so let us sell it to you. We will sign the contract at a certain estate. You should go there first to wait for us." On that day, the king indeed arrived at the estate in a carriage with red wheels and ornate hubs. The person led the way, bringing the craftsman into the estate. The king sat facing south, and the craftsman knelt to ask for the contract to be written. The king nodded, ordered his attendants to write it, and personally signed and stamped it. He first demanded a down payment of three thousand taels of silver, with the remainder to be settled on the day of exchange. The person accompanied the craftsman into the city to hand over the silver and also demanded a fee, giving three hundred taels upfront and agreeing to settle the rest on a future date. On that day, the master craftsman led people to dismantle the hall, but the gate official rejected them.

    449

    匠告以故，门官入白王，王召匠人，仰观王貌，非前日之人也，知为拐骇。无言而退。有衣冠华丽者，乘车带仆至质库，脱金手镯二以质钱。掌柜人细阅之，黄赤无伪，称各重五两。问所需，京钱五百贯，掌柜人还之；其人让至三百贯。北地尚钱帖，如数给之而去。旁一丐者，脱其破袄质二十贯，掌柜人叱之。丐笑曰：“假金镯当钱三百贯，我袄虽破烂，尚非赝物，何不值二十贯耶？”掌柜人心疑，复阅其镯，则已被易包金者。问丐何以知之，丐曰：“此有名骗子手，我知其寓处。

    Explanation

    449

    The craftsman informed the gatekeeper of the reason, and the gatekeeper entered to report to the king. The king summoned the craftsman and looked up at his appearance, realizing it was not the same person as before, and knew something shocking had happened. The craftsman left without speaking. A well-dressed man arrived at the pawnshop with a servant, removed two gold bracelets to pawn for money. The shopkeeper carefully examined them, confirming they were genuine gold and each weighed five taels. The man asked for 500 strings of cash, but the shopkeeper reduced it to 300 strings. The northern region used paper money, and the man was given the equivalent amount and left. A beggar then arrived and pawned his torn coat for 20 strings of cash. The shopkeeper scolded him. The beggar laughed and said, "Fake gold bracelets are worth 300 strings of cash, but my coat, though torn, is not fake. Why is it only worth 20 strings?" The shopkeeper became suspicious and re-examined the bracelets, discovering they had been replaced with fake gold. He asked the beggar how he knew, and the beggar replied, "This is a notorious swindler, and I know his residence."

    450

    ”掌柜人愿给丐钱两贯，偕往寻之，至寓，果见其车在外，丐遥指其人，得钱脱身去矣。掌柜人入寓，则见其与显者共饮，未敢喧哗。因寓主通其仆，唤之出，与之辩论，其人曰：“物既伪，何以质钱如此之多？明是汝换我也。”互相争执。显者闻声邀二人入，笑调其人曰：“我辈宁吃亏，勿占便宜，不可与市井之徒较量，有失官礼。足下钱尚未用，何不还之。”其人似不得已，委屈听命，乃以原钱帖赎还二镯，掌柜人欣然领去。至晚至钱局取钱，则已取去。出其帖比对后帖，系好手描摹者。复至寓，则去已久矣，丐亦不知所往。

    450

    Translation:

    The shopkeeper was willing to give the beggar two strings of cash, and they went together to look for the person. Upon arriving at the inn, they indeed saw his carriage outside. The beggar pointed to the person from afar, and after receiving the money, he quickly left. The shopkeeper entered the inn and saw the person drinking with a prominent individual. He did not dare to make a fuss. Through the innkeeper's mediation, he called out the person's servant, and they argued. The person said, “The item is fake; why did he pledge so much money? It’s clear you switched it with mine.” They argued back and forth. The prominent person heard the commotion and invited the two inside, smilingly teasing him and saying, “We would rather take a loss than gain an advantage. We cannot quarrel with a commoner, as it would be inappropriate for an official. Your money has not been used; why not return it?” The person seemed unwillingly and reluctantly obeyed, returning the two bracelets with the original pledge note. The shopkeeper happily took them away. By evening, he went to the money bureau to collect the money, but it had already been taken. He showed the pledge note and compared it with a later one, finding it to be a well-crafted imitation. He returned to the inn, but the person had already left for a long time. The beggar also did not know where he went.

    451

    有京卿恶其子之不肖而逐之者，其子不知所之。后京卿出为方伯，入庙行香。其时府县以下，侍应者数十员，士庶环观者数百人。突有衣冠破烂者，至方伯前，长跪号哭曰：“儿情愿改过，任凭父亲处置，再不敢稍有违犯，伏望收留。”叩首无算。方伯细察之，非其子也，大怒叱曰：“何处匪徒，敢冒吾子，殊属大胆！”饬役加以缧绁，面交首府问罪。

    Explanation

    451

    There was a high-ranking official in the capital who hated his son's lack of virtue and expelled him. The son did not know where to go. Later, the official was appointed as a provincial governor and entered the temple to burn incense. At that time, there were dozens of officials below the level of prefect and county magistrate who were attending to him, and hundreds of commoners were watching around. Suddenly, a person in tattered clothing appeared before the provincial governor, kneeling for a long time and weeping and crying, saying, "I am willing to reform, and I will accept whatever punishment my father deems fit. I will never dare to violate again. I humbly ask to be taken in." He knelt and bowed his head countless times. The provincial governor, upon closer inspection, realized that it was not his son and became furious, shouting, "Who is this rascal who dares to impersonate my son? This is particularly reckless!" He ordered his servants to bind the person with chains and hand him over to the head of the local government for punishment.

    452

    首府带署讯之，其人供称，前因不好读书，不受训饬，偶有触犯，被逐在外，只求为之挽回，情愿奋勉用功，不敢稍惰，有负恩隆。首府请之家世，言之凿凿，且察其神色悲惨，语言明爽，似非作伪者。试以学问，亦能作文，信其为方伯之逐子也。留之府署，易其衣冠，公余为方伯委婉导之。方伯曰：“实非我子，若无为匪情事，任君遣去可也。”首府归与众官议之，众皆请见其人，问其志向，则凭拜涕泣曰：“父性严厉，己实不才，唯有回籍应试。倘得功名，以赎前衍，恨无旅资，奈何？”众怜其志而哀其遇，遂为资助数百金，送之去，而以责逐复方伯也。后闻家人言，始知伪而非方伯子也。

    Explanation

    452

    The governor brought the man to his office for questioning, and the man confessed that he had previously been unwilling to study and did not obey discipline, occasionally violating rules and being expelled from the household. He only wished to be forgiven and was willing to work hard, not wanting to be lazy and disappoint his father's grace. The governor inquired about his family background, and the man spoke clearly and convincingly. Observing his sad expression and clear, straightforward speech, it seemed he was not lying. When tested on his knowledge, he was also able to write, leading the governor to believe he was the son who had been expelled. The governor kept him in his office, provided him with new clothes, and after official duties, gently guided him. The official said, "He is not my son; if he has not committed any crimes, you may send him away." The governor then discussed the matter with other officials, who all requested to meet the man. When they asked about his aspirations, he knelt and wept, saying, "My father is strict, and I am indeed unworthy. The only thing I can do is return home to take the exams. If I can achieve success, I will atone for my past mistakes, but I regret not having travel funds, what shall I do?" The officials pitied his determination and felt sorry for his situation, so they provided him with several hundred gold pieces and sent him away, holding the official responsible for expelling him. Later, hearing from the family, they realized he was pretending and not the official's son.

    453

    越人倪某，世习申韩，由幕而官，去官复幕，在保阳待聘，住旅寓有时矣。迫切钻营，无微不至。是年冬，寓中内厅宅，有人来往，章服华焕，仆从趋跄，往来之客，络绎不绝，似皆宪司之纪纲也。倪询其仆，知为大名府总司阍，奉本官命，来省置办衣饰，为少君完姻，并延访善于刑名钱谷之士，以辞旧友者。倪探得其情，即具衣冠往拜。其人稍闲，即与盘桓，竟成莫逆。知其性情慷爽，而目不识丁。一夜，漏已三下，突有役来，传本官谕帖，催其人归去。其人得信，叩倪寝门而告之，曰：“请先生起，烦为一读来书。

    Explanation

    453

    A man from Yue named Ni, who had studied the doctrines of Shen and Han, rose from being a retainer to becoming an official, then left his official position to return to being a retainer. He was waiting in Baoyang for a new appointment and had been staying at an inn for some time. He was urgently striving and scheming, attending to every detail. In that winter, in the inner hall of his inn, there were people coming and going, all dressed in splendid official attire, with servants moving briskly. Guests were coming and going incessantly, and they seemed to be bound by the regulations of the censorate. Ni inquired of his servant and learned that they were the chief gatekeeper of Daming Prefecture, who had come on orders from his official to purchase clothing and ornaments for the young lady, arrange her marriage, and seek out scholars skilled in criminal law, money, and grain management, who were bidding farewell to old friends. Ni discovered his intentions and, dressed in formal attire, went to pay his respects. When the man had a moment of leisure, they lingered together and eventually became close friends. Ni knew that he was a man of open and generous character but illiterate. One night, after the third strike of the night bell, suddenly a servant came to deliver the official's written order, urging him to return. The man received the message, knocked on Ni's bedroom door, and informed him, saying, "Please rise, sir, and trouble you to read the letter."

    454

    有役守候，不能待旦矣。”倪起而诵之，不过因办公乏人，谕令速将什物置妥即去；并旧友辞定，所访之新友，必须于开篆以前到署等语。其人踟蹰曰：“办物不难，访友非易，先生意中有信托之人乎？”倪遂自述其历就州县，助其东人升迁者不一。其人笑曰：“吾以先生为记室耳，畴知申韩妙手，近在邻居，而犹外求人耶。”即请代某廪复云：“以千金订定倪先生，俟各物办齐即回，不敢逗留也。”倪为书就，交役竟去。次日，其人送关书聘仪来，请安侍立，不敢抗礼。其仆从咸来叩喜，倪大悦。扯其人入坐，曰：“相好在前，既承不弃，断不可以常情拘也。

    Explanation

    454

    There was a servant on duty, unable to wait until dawn. Ni rose and recited it, which was no more than a formal instruction to quickly arrange items and depart; and to bid farewell to old friends and ensure that the new friends visited before the official ceremony began. The person hesitated and said, "Arranging items is not difficult, but visiting friends is not easy. Do you have a trusted person in mind?" Ni then replied, "I thought you were just a secretary, who knew that the master of Shang and Han was right next door and you were still looking for someone else?" He immediately asked to write a reply on behalf of a certain granary, saying, "To engage Mr. Ni with a thousand gold pieces, and he will return once everything is arranged, not staying longer." Ni wrote the reply and handed it over to the servant, who then left. The next day, the person brought the contract and gift, and respectfully stood waiting, not daring to be overly familiar. His servants all came to congratulate, and Ni was very pleased. He pulled the person to sit down and said, "Given our long friendship, since you have not abandoned me, we should not be bound by ordinary conventions."

    455

    ”其人称谢侧坐，告以首饰尚未造完，俟工竣先归，当遣车奉迓耳。又数日，见倪似有不豫之色。倪惶然叩故，其人曰：“贵乡亲某号缎局内，看定货物，不过偶挂数百金耳，艰持不舍，何其吝也。”倪曰：“方侩恒情，何足怪者。我与素识，代为作保，虽千金亦不靳矣。”其人曰：“如此固好，恐先生未之真信耳。”倪曰：“忝在同署，尚何疑，是足下多心矣。”即偕赴缎局，所定之物，实千余金，仅付百两，余皆挂欠；故未肯与。然局主已访得大名确有姻事，及倪就其幕。故倪至，殷勤款接。

    Explanation

    455

    The person bowed and sat aside, explaining that the jewelry had not yet been completed and that he would return once the work was finished, promising to send a carriage to escort him. A few days later, it seemed that Ni was in a gloomy mood. Ni, feeling anxious, asked the reason. The person said, "Your esteemed relative, Mr. So-and-so, who owns a silk shop, had agreed on the goods but only temporarily held back a few hundred taels of silver, showing a reluctance to part with it—how stingy it is!" Ni replied, "It is the usual practice of merchants; it is not surprising. I have known him for a long time and will vouch for him; even if it were a thousand taels, I would not hesitate." The person said, "That is good, but I fear you may not fully trust me." Ni said, "Being in the same office, how can one doubt? You are being overly cautious." They then went together to the silk shop. The goods that had been agreed upon were actually worth more than a thousand taels, but only a hundred taels had been paid, with the rest on credit; hence, they were unwilling to hand them over. However, the owner of the shop had learned that you have a confirmed marriage arrangement and that Ni is working in his office. Therefore, when Ni arrived, he was received warmly and hospitably.

    456

    其人笑曰：“尔铺主不我信耶？请以先生为质。明年来请先生时，其价原车带到，决无贻误。”倪亦言其就事之故，愿力任之。局主欣然交其货物，给其人满载而去。次年，俟至春仲，无车来接。局主怀疑，邀倪同往大名。问司阍，并无其人，亦无易幕事。倪归，货其行李以偿铺帐，悔恨无及。江右某宰相，保举其门人某为江南中丞者，某感恩戴德，思有以报之。适相之次公子，以书来干谒。中丞见之，其人少年倜傥，应对如流。缘书中有告助意，问其所需，以二千金为请。中丞允之，请与盘桓，则以父命迫促为辞。

    Explanation

    456

    The man laughed and said, "Does your shop owner not trust me? Please let me be a guarantor for you. When you come to fetch Mr. Ni next year, the price will be paid in full by the vehicle, and there will be no delay." Ni also explained the reason for taking the job and expressed his willingness to take responsibility. The manager gladly handed over the goods, and the man left fully loaded. The following year, by mid-spring, no vehicle came to fetch him. The manager became suspicious and invited Ni to go to Daming together. They asked the gatekeeper, who said there was no such person, and there were no changes in the office. Ni returned, selling his belongings to pay off the shop's debts, and regretted it deeply. A certain chancellor from Jiangxi recommended one of his disciples as the governor of Jiangnan, who was grateful and wanted to repay him. Coincidentally, the chancellor's second son came with a letter to seek an audience. The governor saw him, and the young man was charming and eloquent. Since the letter mentioned a request for help, the governor asked what he needed, and he requested two thousand taels of silver. The governor agreed, and asked to keep him for a while, but the young man excused himself with the urgency of his father's orders.

    457

    及回拜，则见其巨舫悬旌，行李仆从，莫不炫耀。中丞意师相素尚俭朴，而其子不类，心有所疑；其幕中有同门某孝廉，以疑质之。孝廉曰：“某公子，予幼曾见之，今相隔有年矣。”出其所书与扇，中丞观之，楷法挺秀，笑谓孝廉，曰：“明日我宴公子，屈阁下作陪，面索其书，则真伪立辨矣。”乃设席请公子。公子来见孝廉，先呼曰：“兄台相隔多年，尚识鄙人否？”孝廉不能辨，唯唯而已。

    Explanation

    457

    Upon returning to pay respects, the Chancellor saw a large boat with banners hanging, and his entourage of traveling companions and servants were all showing off. The Chancellor, who had always admired the simplicity and frugality of the Prime Minister, was puzzled by the behavior of his son. There was a fellow student and scholar-official in his entourage, and he questioned him about his doubts. The scholar-official said, "That young master, I saw him when I was young. We have been apart for many years." He then produced a fan with a piece of writing on it. The Chancellor examined it and found the calligraphy to be elegant and well-formed. He smiled and said to the scholar-official, "Tomorrow I will host a banquet for the young master. I invite you to join as a companion. If I ask him to write something in person, we can immediately distinguish between truth and falsehood." So he set up a feast to invite the young master. When the young master came to see the scholar-official, he first called out, "Brother, after so many years, do you still recognize me?" The scholar-official could not tell, and he simply nodded in response.

    458

    中丞出纸求书，公子欣然允诺，命仆研磨，其仆面赤手战，目视公子，公子吟哦联句，提笔欲书，忽掷笔叱仆曰：“盍去诸！彼慢我矣。乘我有所干求之际，故索我书，彼乃以卖字之文丐视我耶？”扬长出外，登舆而去。中丞追送之，亦不回顾。中丞自悔鲁莽，具黄金二百，至其舟中谢罪，强之收受而去。未几，又一中堂之幼子来，与前人名柬相同，并无书札。见之，则朴素暗淡，恂恂儒雅，使孝廉相客，客不认识。留之入署，亦愿往；索其书法，亦无辞。中丞狐疑莫解，使捷足入都探之，始知前者入骗子手矣。浙有洋商金姓者，送其子侄应童子试，寓学院之东辕外。有人叩门请见，金见之，衣冠华焕，仆从甚都，访问书生陈某有无。

    Explanation

    458

    The Vice-Chancellor presented paper and requested calligraphy, and the young master gladly agreed. He ordered his servant to grind the ink, but the servant, pale-faced and trembling, looked at the young master. The young master recited and composed poetry, picked up his brush to write, but suddenly threw down the brush and shouted at the servant, "Why don't you leave! He is disrespectful to me. Taking advantage of my request, he asks for my calligraphy, treating me as a beggar for my writing?" The young master left in a grand manner, getting into his carriage and departing. The Vice-Chancellor chased after him, but the young master did not look back. Regretting his hasty actions, the Vice-Chancellor prepared two hundred gold pieces and went to the young master's boat to apologize, forcing him to accept the gift before leaving. Not long after, another young son of a high-ranking official arrived, with a name similar to the first, but no letter was sent. When the young master saw him, he was simple, subdued, and scholarly. He sent a scholar-official to meet the guest, but the guest did not recognize him. The young master invited the guest to his office, and the guest agreed, but when asked for calligraphy, the guest refused. The Vice-Chancellor was confused and sent someone to investigate, only to discover that the first encounter was a scam. In Zhejiang, there was a foreign merchant named Jin, who sent his nephew to take the children's examination, staying outside the eastern gate of the academy. Someone knocked on the door to request an audience, and the merchant saw a well-dressed visitor with a large retinue, who asked about a scholar named Chen.

    459

    先是金与陈系中表，知其向在江南游幕者，其时实未回籍，乃告以故。叩其来访之意，其人踌躇曰：“陈君不来，事不谐矣。我施姓，东南某科乙榜，与陈君至好。我为学使所聘来此阅卷者。”遂告别而出。门有肩舆，施登舆，由东辕直入仪门去矣。金闻学使向有货取之名，疑来人与陈必有勾串事，然亦无从进门矣，姑置之。一日，偕亲友遨游西子湖，于圣因寺前遇施。同行两客，皆翩翩年少，从事辈三四人，随有舆马。

    Explanation

    459

    Previously, Jin and Chen were related by marriage. Jin knew that Chen had been traveling in the south as a guest in someone's household, and at that time, he had not yet returned to his hometown. Then, he informed Jin of the reason. Jin inquired about the purpose of his visit, and the person hesitated and said, "If Chen does not come, the matter will not succeed." The person introduced himself, saying, "My surname is Shi, and I am a certain rank in the southeastern examination. I was hired by the provincial examiner to come here to examine the papers." Then he bid farewell and left. There was a sedan chair at the door, and Shi entered it, going directly through the eastern gate and the ceremonial gate.

    Jin heard that the provincial examiner had a reputation for buying things and suspected that the visitor and Chen must have some kind of conspiracy, but there was no way to enter the gate, so he put it aside. One day, he went with friends and relatives to enjoy the West Lake, and he met Shi in front of the Shengyin Temple. Two guests were traveling together, both were young and graceful, accompanied by three or four attendants, followed by sedan chairs and horses.

    460

    遥见金来，施出队趋迎，握手道故，指一衣秋香绉袄带盘金便帽者曰：“此学使之大公子也。”又指一卵色纺衫者曰：“此某孝廉，我同年同事也。”又谓二人曰：“此金长者，系我至友。”遂问金同行亲友之姓名，互相揖让。施大笑曰：“难得英俊多人，不期而会。我合作东道主，畅叙一日矣！”邀入五柳居，登楼列座。酒家以木板来，指点酒肴之精妙者，次第供应。施与孝廉，纵谈古人，议论文墨，公子殷勤劝金浮大白。酒正酣，苍头飞马而来曰：“大人有命，请公子同归。”金暗起给资，酒家曰：“上座之客，入门时已先付讫。”金局促不安。

    Explanation

    460

    From afar, Shi saw Jin approaching and rushed out to greet him, shaking hands and exchanging old stories. He pointed to a man wearing a light yellow silk robe with a gold-embroidered cap and said, "This is the eldest son of the scholar-official." Then he pointed to another man wearing a pale yellow cotton robe and said, "This is a certain scholar-official, a contemporary colleague of mine." He then told the two men, "This is Jin, a respected gentleman and a close friend of mine." Shi then asked about Jin's traveling companions and friends, and they bowed to each other. Shi laughed heartily and said, "It's rare to meet so many talented and handsome people unexpectedly. I will host as the local host and we can chat freely for a whole day!" He invited them to Five Willow Hall, went upstairs, and arranged seats. The wine shop owner brought a wooden board, pointed out the best dishes and drinks, and served them in order. Shi and the scholar-official talked freely about ancient people and discussed literature, while the young master warmly urged Jin to drink a large cup of wine. As the wine was just getting warm, a messenger on horseback came running and said, "The master has a command, please return with the young master." Jin secretly prepared money, but the wine shop owner said, "The guests who sat at the upper seats had already paid when they entered." Jin felt uneasy and restless.

    461

    施等皆下楼，谓金曰：“三生有幸，始得定交，如蒙相思，只须告把门张老，我自来耳。”作别乘舆乘马，纷纷而散。金归与子侄言之，佥参赞作报琼之举，以申敬意。遂于旅邸盛设，具柬交张老以邀三君子。次日，施来赴宴，大公子与孝廉，皆为公事所羁，不能共酌，心甚歉然，嘱某致谢。遂入席，请金之子侄皆出，索窗课阅之，曰：“佳则佳矣，但与学使风气，尚不甚合。就文论之，即如某篇，某处应提，某处应顿，结以大尾，则投学使之好，无不命中矣。”咸服其论。金乃重伸陈君之说，究为何事。施曰：“尔我既成相好，不妨实告。

    Explanation

    461

    Shi and others all went downstairs and said to Jin, "We are extremely fortunate to finally establish a firm friendship. If you think of me, just tell the gatekeeper, Old Zhang, and I will come myself." They bade farewell and went away in carriages and on horseback, dispersing in a scattered manner. Jin returned home and told his nephews about it. They all agreed to participate in a gesture of returning jade as a token of gratitude to express their respect. They then prepared a grand feast at the inn and prepared a formal invitation to be delivered to Old Zhang to invite the three gentlemen. The next day, Shi came to the banquet. The eldest son and the scholar-official were both occupied with official duties and could not join in the drinking, feeling very apologetic. They entrusted someone to express their thanks. They then took their seats and asked Jin's nephews to step out. Shi asked to see the window lessons and said, "It is good, but it does not quite match the style of the examiner. In terms of the text, for example, in a certain passage, certain parts should be emphasized, and certain parts should be paused, and it should end with a strong conclusion. Then it will surely hit the mark of the examiner." Everyone was convinced by his argument. Jin then asked again about what Tchen had said, wanting to know exactly what it was. Shi said, "Since we have become good friends, I may as well tell you the truth."

    462

    学使之大公子，好与人相接，嘱我辈为之介绍。陈君前约，贵处之某侄，通邑富豪，欲与公子纳交，不意陈君逗留不至，实为缺兴。”金曰：“如我子侄，不识可以充数乎？”施曰：“无不可者，但缟之费，各需千金，能乎否乎？”金曰：“得半之道，尚可勉力。”施难之。金再三恳切，既而曰：“幸有我在，或可商办。”言未已，有一役以提督学院大灯来迎。金送出门，见其由中道入，文武巡官，皆中立候进。金深信不疑。翌晨，施来，曰：“关防在迩，迟恐不得出。兹大公子以我故，屈允所请，须面封礼物，榜发来取。”相与同赴钱局，如数兑银，公封而回，给以关节。

    Explanation

    462

    The eldest son of the provincial governor, who enjoys socializing and has asked us to introduce him to others, had previously arranged for a meeting with a wealthy merchant from a nearby town, one of your nephews. However, Mr. Chen remained and did not come, causing disappointment. Gold said, "Can my nephews or sons be suitable?" The other person replied, "Nothing is impossible, but the cost of silk gifts is a thousand gold pieces each. Can you afford it?" Gold said, "I can manage half the amount, but I will do my best." The other person was hesitant, but after Gold's repeated earnest requests, he said, "Fortunately, I am here, and we can perhaps arrange it." Before he finished speaking, a servant arrived with a lantern to greet them. Gold saw the procession enter through the main gate, with both civil and military officials standing in the middle to wait for them. Gold had no doubts about the authenticity of the event. The next morning, the other person came and said, "The deadline is approaching, and if we delay, we might not be able to leave. The governor's eldest son, because of me, has agreed to the request. We need to seal the gifts in person and post a notice to collect them." They went together to the treasury, exchanged the silver, sealed it officially, and returned, giving each other the necessary connections.

    463

    未几试毕，金之子侄，皆落孙山外，始疑之。赴局开兑，则原封不动，而易以砖石矣。内有一纸书云：“大宗师如此清正，汝曹妄想功名，理应重罚。所封千金，权借济急，销汝罪衍。以佛法论之，或者来世有奉还之日，未可知也。不必冤曲好人。此嘱。”金大怒，纳交巡官，以访其事，始知学使幕中，实无施姓，即大公子亦年貌不符。细揣其故，系骗子先冒杂役，放水菜时入内，其时号舍无人，藏匿其中，易衣冠而出。巡官见其华焕，且自内出，则拱候之；其入亦然。

    Explanation

    463

    Not long after the examination ended, Gold's nephews and grandsons all failed, and he began to suspect something was amiss. When he went to the office to open the deposit, he found it sealed and untouched, but it had been replaced with bricks and stones. Inside was a note that read: "The Great Master is so upright and incorruptible. You lot, who wish for fame and fortune, should be severely punished. The thousand gold you deposited is temporarily borrowed to help in an emergency, to atone for your sins. From the perspective of Buddhist teachings, perhaps there will be a day in the next life when it can be returned, though that is uncertain. Do not wrong good people. This is my advice." Gold was furious and reported the matter to the patrol officer, who investigated and found out that there was no one named Shi in the examiner's entourage, and even the eldest son did not match in age and appearance. After careful consideration, they realized that the fraudster had first pretended to be a miscellaneous worker, entering when the water and vegetables were being released. At that time, the exam room was empty, and he hid inside, changing clothes and coming out. The patrol officer saw his splendid appearance and, since he came out from inside, greeted him politely; the same happened when he entered.

    464

    入则仍易破衣，由杂役中出矣。至把门张老，及灯笼夫，皆其党役充者，榜后俱逸。所骗亦不仅金姓一处也。有贵公子挟重资游姑苏以买妾者，官媒唤来数十人，皆不合意。一老妪随舆而来，曰：“郎君法眼过高，此等人皆不中选，非我姨家瑶仙大姑不能如愿，惜身价过高耳。”公子闻之曰：“如果真正佳人，何妨重价。第恐有名无实耳。汝姑带来一观。”妪笑曰：“我知郎君，只好看瘦马婢耳。清白人家，即穷至不吃饭，何肯将娇女送与人看耶？”公子谢过，愿同往访之。

    Explanation

    464

    When he entered, he still wore tattered clothes, emerging from among the miscellaneous tasks. The old man who guards the gate and the lantern bearer were all filled with his party members, and after the announcement, they all escaped. The deception was not only in one place of the Jin family. There was a noble young man carrying heavy wealth traveling to Suzhou to buy a concubine. The official matchmaker called over dozens of people, but all were unsatisfactory. An old woman followed the carriage and said, "Young master's discerning eye is too high; these people are all not selected. Only my aunt's niece,瑶仙, can fulfill your wish, but it's just that her price is too high." The young man replied, "If she is truly a beautiful woman, why not pay a high price? Only fear that there is a name but no substance. Bring her to see for yourself." The old woman laughed and said, "I know young master; you only look at thin horses and maidservants. In a clean and upright household, even if they are poor to the point of not eating, how would they send their beautiful daughter to be seen by others?" The young man thanked her and wished to go with her to visit.

    465

    妪曰：“我试言之，不知人家愿否？”公子许重给媒资，妪请姑探之。隔五六日，公子望眼几穿，妪始欣欣然来，曰：“凭我一片舌，煞费苦心，肯与郎君一面矣。”遂往观之。其女一拜而退，娉婷之态，秀丽之容，公子已神魂欲堕。旋闻琴韵铿然，和以燕语莺声，长吟度曲，公子几入痴魔，亟问值价。妪忙掩其口扯之出曰：“郎君几自误，此女不可唐突，当云聘作亚妻，则其父贪而好名，或可动之。既至尔家，则任分嫡庶矣。”又言聘只千金，女须衣饰，尚须彩舆迎入，缺一不能成也。公子已心醉，无不从命，乃立婚书，纳聘约吉迎娶。

    Explanation

    465

    The matchmaker said, "Let me try to say it; I don't know if the family will agree." The young man promised to give generously, and the matchmaker asked her to go and check. After five or six days, the young man's eyes nearly popped out from staring so intently, and the matchmaker returned cheerfully, saying, "With my tongue, I've gone to great lengths, and she is willing to meet the young master." She then went to see her. The young woman bowed once and left, her graceful posture and beautiful appearance leaving the young man almost in a trance. Soon after, he heard the clear sound of the qin, accompanied by the soft chirping of birds, and she sang a long melody. The young man was almost overcome by love and urgently asked about the price. The matchmaker quickly covered his mouth and pulled him out, saying, "Young master, you almost made a mistake. This girl cannot be treated disrespectfully; you should propose to marry her as a secondary wife. Her father is greedy and fond of reputation, so he might be persuaded. Once she comes to your home, you can decide whether she is the principal or secondary wife." She also mentioned that the dowry was only a thousand gold pieces, but the girl needed clothing and accessories, and a colorful carriage to bring her in; if any one of these is missing, the marriage cannot be completed. The young man was already deeply infatuated and agreed to everything, so they signed the marriage contract, paid the dowry, arranged the auspicious date, and went to fetch her.

    466

    公子雇巨舫，作洞房，欲仿范蠡载西子游五湖故事，先买一婢以俟。至日，彩舆迎来，妪与婢扶新人入舱坐，妪乘闹逸去。公子揭去新人面巾，神色焕然，唯不言不动。爰设度遣婢，公子亲手扶之，新人失跌，其声秃然，以火照之，乃庙中木偶耳。急遣人追至女家，则门锁闭。访诸邻，曰：“是家偶赁此宅以嫁女，兹已送女去，不知所之。”问诸官媒，无识妪者。计花费千余金，仅载二粗婢，索然而归。姑苏阊门外，通衢大道，百货交集之区，而人参行尤盛。间有空宅，亦甚宽广。时有服四品衣冠者，迁于空宅，门悬候补府陈姓封条。

    Explanation

    466

    The young nobleman hired a large boat and prepared a bridal chamber, intending to imitate the legendary story of Fan Lǐ and Xi Shi traveling around the Five Lakes. He first bought a maid in advance to prepare for the occasion. On the appointed day, a decorated carriage brought the bride, and an old woman and a maid helped her into the cabin and seated her. The old woman took advantage of the commotion to slip away. The nobleman then removed the bride's veil, revealing a radiant appearance, but she remained silent and motionless. He arranged to send the maid away and personally helped the bride, who suddenly stumbled and fell with a sharp sound. Upon lighting a fire to examine her, he discovered that she was a wooden statue from a temple. Quickly, he sent someone to chase after the girl's family, but found the door locked. Visiting the neighbors, he learned that the family had temporarily rented the house to marry off their daughter and had already sent her away, without knowing her whereabouts. He also inquired of the matchmakers, but no one recognized the old woman. In the end, he spent over a thousand gold pieces and only brought back two rough maids, returning home empty-handed. Outside the Chuan Gate in Suzhou, on a busy thoroughfare where many goods were gathered, the ginseng trade was especially prosperous. Occasionally, there were empty houses, which were also quite spacious. At times, a high-ranking official wearing the attire of the fourth rank would move into one of these empty houses, with the door hung with a seal indicating it belonged to a waiting official named Chen.

    467

    其司阍司帐司厨及侍从之仆数十人，出入乘四人舆，张红盖，现任之府州厅县，佥鸣钲开道来拜。时亦宴客，舆马盈门，参行人见之屡参。一日，有二仆衣履鲜华，相率至各参行，阅货问价。行主叩其主，则曰：“西人也，为陈天官之长公子，以荫生加纳太守，分发江苏候补者。家资亿万，举家皆嗜人参，以代茶饮，前带来参将尽矣，主人命我等选择公平之家，以便长可交易。”于是行主争趋其仆。而仆游十余家，皆不合意。是时，参业中专有陪宾之伙，已遍传各行。

    Explanation

    467

    In the household of the main personage, there were dozens of servants, including doorkeepers, accountants, cooks, and attendants. Whenever he entered or left, he rode in a sedan chair carried by four people, with a red canopy over it. Officials from various administrative levels, including prefectures, states, halls, and counties, would sound bells and clear the way as they came to pay their respects. Sometimes, he would host guests, and the gates would be filled with carriages and horses, with officials repeatedly paying their respects. One day, two servants dressed in fine attire visited various shops to inspect goods and ask about prices. The shopkeepers asked who their master was, and the servants replied, "We are Westerners, the eldest sons of a high-ranking official (Tian Guan), who inherited their position as prefects and were assigned to wait for vacancies in Jiangsu Province. Our family has immense wealth, and everyone in the household loves ginseng, using it as a substitute for tea. Our previous supply of ginseng has been exhausted, and our master has instructed us to find fair merchants so that we can conduct business." The shopkeepers rushed to serve the servants. However, the servants visited more than ten shops but found none satisfactory. At that time, the ginseng trade had specialized assistants who had already spread the news to various shops.

    468

    故二仆远至一里之外，行主者皆知之，情愿许二仆重扣。仆许，行主遣伙持参同去其寓，先称一两试尝之，价值三百余，即与宝银七提。其伙回，侈张公馆内之华丽，且探知其太夫人，每日需服参三钱，一年有十数万金交易。其眷属不日到矣。行主甚悦。未几，其主艳服乘舆而来，谓行主曰：“尔家货真价实。我太夫人已到，常吃好参，须至佳者。”行主奉以顶上参，称四十两，命一伙携参去兑银，且曰：“兑齐后，遣工人抬送银封可也。”二伙相从至馆舍，登堂入室，旋达后楼。其主以后楼为房，房内罗帐高悬，锦衾绣缛，洋表时钟之属，陈设焕然。

    Explanation

    468

    So the two servants went as far as one mile away, and all the innkeepers knew about it. They were willing to allow the two servants a higher price. The servant agreed, and the innkeeper sent a helper with ginseng to their lodging. First, they weighed one liang to try it, and it was valued at over three hundred. Immediately, they gave seven taels of precious silver. The helper returned, boasting about the splendor of the public hall and discovering that her grandmother was there, requiring three qian of ginseng daily, with tens of thousands of gold transactions annually. Her family members would arrive soon. The innkeeper was very pleased. Soon, the lady came in splendid attire and in a carriage, telling the innkeeper, "Your goods are genuine and worth the price. My grandmother has arrived, and she often eats good ginseng; it must be the best." The innkeeper presented the top-grade ginseng, weighing forty liang, and ordered a helper to take the ginseng to exchange for silver, saying, "After the exchange is complete, send workers to carry the sealed silver." The two helpers followed them to the inn, entered the main hall, and then reached the rear building. The lady used the rear building as her room, where inside there were high-hanging silk curtains, embroidered bedding, and European-style clocks and watches, all arranged beautifully.

    469

    其箱箧以四为式，自床东直至窗前数十号。乃命仆开第五排，二十号贴地一箱。正拆银封秤兑，忽楼下有人操西音，大呼而来，曰：“今日虎邱之东，奈何不赴？我寻将来也！”其主谓二伙曰：“客且坐，此我乡亲某刺史也。其向我借贷屡矣，不可使彼登楼，见如许物，则更扰累不清矣。”使仆以参及银皆归箱内锁之，匆匆下楼，闻彼来客强拉之行。仆来送茶，传其主命，曰：“烦客略坐守，去即来矣。”乃反扣楼门而去。

    Explanation

    469

    The chests and boxes were arranged in sets of four, extending from the east side of the bed to the window, with several dozen numbered boxes. The owner then instructed the servant to open the fifth row, specifically the twenty-first box placed on the ground. Just as they were opening the silver seal and weighing the contents, a sudden shout came from downstairs, with someone speaking in a Western accent. "Why didn't you come to Tiger Hill today? I'm coming to find you!" the visitor exclaimed. The owner told his companions, "Guest, please sit. This is my fellow townsman, a local official. He has borrowed money from me many times before, and I cannot let him see the contents of these boxes, as it would cause even more trouble." He ordered the servant to put everything back into the box and lock it, then hurried downstairs. He heard the visitor insistently pulling him away. The servant returned with tea and conveyed the owner's message: "Please wait a moment, guest. I will be right back." The visitor then closed the door behind him and left.

    470

    旋闻幼仆数人，在下戏谑，始而喧哗，继而揪斗；有老苍头来吆喝，不应，鞭挞从之，幼仆不服，哭声震耳。久之寂然。至晚，无一人来，二伙馁甚，推窗望楼下，适行主同伙伴持灯唤入。二伙应曰：“勿庸着慌，人参与银俱在此。”行主登楼，去扣入门，以火照之，二伙指此箱，曰参银都在内也。行主曰：“予自大门至楼，人物一空，似已迁去。不妨开其箱。”遂共观之，洞见楼下。细揣其箱底，与地板凿通，触机运转。遍举各物，无甚贵重者，除罗帐外，其衾缛系高丽纸印洋花者；钟表仅有外面，中空无物；箱皆纸糊，中藏石块数包而已。始悟诸仆叫唤争斗时，正转运箱内之物，以人声嘈杂混之，俾不觉。

    Explanation

    470

    Soon after, several young servants were heard playing around and teasing each other. At first, they were noisy, and then they started fighting. An old servant came to scold them, but they did not respond, and he beat them with a whip. The young servants refused to submit, and their loud crying could be heard clearly. After a while, it became silent. By evening, no one came, and the two groups were very hungry. They pushed open the window and looked down below, just then, the master and his companions came with a lamp and called them in. The young servants said, "Don't worry, the ginseng and silver are all here." The master went up to the second floor, knocked on the door, and lit a fire to look inside. The young servants pointed to the box and said, "The ginseng and silver are all inside." The master said, "I went from the front door to the second floor, and all the people and things were gone. It seems they have moved away. Let's open the box." They all looked at it and saw clearly down below. They carefully examined the bottom of the box and found that it was connected to the floor by a hole and was triggered to move. They looked through all the items, and nothing was particularly valuable. Apart from the silk canopy, the bedding was decorated with foreign-style flowers printed on Korean paper. The clocks and watches had only their outer casings and were empty inside. The boxes were made of paper and contained several packages of stones. They finally realized that when the servants were shouting and fighting, they were actually moving things inside the box, using the noisy shouting to mask the sound so that it wouldn't be noticed.

    471

    行主鸣诸官，且问与骗子往来之故。官曰：“以都中枢密信来，不能不答。”乃为缉捕，毫无影踪。有耆而聋者，在武大关陵乞丐。关前来一官舫，扬旌鸣钲而泊。舱中有五品官，探首见丐，使从者扶之登舟，官细察之曰：“汝非某长者乎？前曾继我为义子，我因回籍求功名去，今幸选得是邦官，不意义父一贫至此，儿之罪也。”丐知其误，姑应之曰：“我年老糊涂，前事如梦矣。”官曰：“虽系风尘面目，骨格犹存，儿识之无误。

    Explanation

    471

    The head official summoned the various officials and asked about the reason for associating with scoundrels. The official replied, "Based on a secret message from the central authority, I could not fail to respond." They then proceeded to apprehend the target, but found no trace. There was an elderly and deaf man begging at the tomb of Wu Da Guan. At the gate, an official boat arrived, its banners raised and gongs sounding as it came to anchor. In the cabin, a fifth-rank official peered out and saw the beggar. He ordered his attendants to help the beggar onto the boat, then carefully examined him and said, "Are you not a certain elder? You once adopted me as your son. I returned to my hometown to seek fame and achievement, and now I am fortunate to be appointed as an official in this state. I didn’t expect my adoptive father to be so poor—it is my fault." The beggar knew he was mistaken and provisionally replied, "I am old and confused; the past is like a dream." The official said, "Although your face is weathered by the world, your bones and features still remain—I recognize you without mistake."

    472

    ”饬从者请封翁先付浴堂沐浴更衣，移舟至僻静处所，颐养月余，为之栉理须发，暗以胶粉染之，皤然一叟。谓曰：“儿衣不称父身，将入市买金帛，为父修饰，以便同赴任所。但父虽在此行乞，恐城中有识者，碍儿颜面，至铺内阅货时，合意，只须摇首，不可多言。”丐允之。放舟入城，唤肩舆二乘，随带二仆，父子皆服五品衣冠，招摇过市。入银楼换金约臂，每个重四两者两对，谓铺主曰：“我将赴缎局，偕往兑银可也。”铺主从之。入缎局，以单与局主观之，须三千余金货物。邀入厅堂，殷勤款接。私叩其仆，知少者为严州二府，老者是其封翁。

    Explanation

    472

    Final Translation:

    Instruct the attendant to bring the old man to the bathhouse for a bath and a change of clothes, then move the boat to a secluded place, where he will be cared for and rested for more than a month. His hair and beard will be combed and styled, and secretly dyed with a mixture of glue and powder, making him appear as a venerable old man. He said, “My clothes do not fit my father’s body. I will go into the city to buy gold and silk, to adorn him, so that we can travel together to the new post. However, even though my father is begging here, I fear that people in the city who know him might embarrass me. When we go into the shop to look at the goods, if anything is to my liking, I will simply shake my head and not speak.” The old man agreed.

    They set off by boat into the city, calling for two sedan chairs and bringing along two servants. Both father and son wore the official dress of a fifth-rank official, parading through the marketplace in a showy manner. They entered a silver shop and exchanged money for armlets, each pair weighing four taels. They told the shopkeeper, “I am going to the silk merchant’s shop; I will go with you to exchange the silver.” The shopkeeper agreed. They went to the silk merchant’s shop, where they showed the order to the merchant, who said they needed goods worth more than three thousand gold coins. They were invited into the main hall and received with great courtesy and hospitality. Privately inquiring of his servants, they learned that the younger man was one of the officials from Yanzhou, and the older man was his father-in-law.

    473
